text: CONGONHAS-DO-CAMPO, a parish and village of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, 10 m. E of Queluz, on the margin of an affluent of the Paraúpéba. Pop. 3,000. There are active iron mines in this district.

CONGREHOY, or Congreiro, a river of Guatemala, falling into the sea on the W coast of Honduras, 11 leagues E of Bishop's-head. About 6 m. inland from its embouchure, a remarkable peak, called C. peak, rises to an estimated alt. of 7,500 ft. This mountain is in N lat. 15° 39', W long. 86° 54'.
CONGRESBURY, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 64 m. NNE of Axbridge, intersected by the river Yeo. Area 4,280 acres. Pop. 1,330.

CONGRESS, a township in Richland co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., on the head-streams of Whetstone river. Pop. 1,248.—Also a township in Wayne co., in the state of Ohio, on Killbuck creek. Pop. 2,006.

CONGUN, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, on the coast of the Persian gulf, 120 m. SSE of Bushire, and about the same distance S by W of Shiraz. It is a large and populous town, and has a considerable trade along the gulf and also with the interior. Pop. estimated at 6,000.

CONHOCTON, a township in Steuben co., in the state of New York, 18 m. NW of Bath. Pop. 2,965. -Also a river of the same co., which, uniting with the Tioga, forms the Chemung river. It has a course of 55 m.

CONI, or Cu'neo, a province of the states of Sar- N by the prov. of Saluzzo; on the E by that of Mondovi; on the S by that of Nice; and on the W by the dep. of Basses-Alpes in France. Its length from E to W is 42 m.; its mean breadth from N to S about 27 m.; and its superficial area 760 Italian sq. m. The Maritime Alps encircle the prov. on the S and W, and cover a large proportion of the superficies with their ramifications. The Stura and the Maira are its principal rivers. The former is united to the Po by a canal which is navigable from Coni to Carmagnola. The valleys, naturally fertile, enjoy the advantage of an excellent system of irrigation by means of numerous streams and canals; but only about one-third of the total surface is under cultivation. The prov. produces corn, maize, wine, fruit, hemp, and silk; and great numbers of cattle are reared. Iron, lead, marble and slate, are quarried; and there are several thermal springs.—The pop. in 1821 was 152,861; in 1839, 168,796.—This prov. is governed by an intendant-general of the 2d class; and is subdivided into 19 mandamentos, viz.: San Borgo, Dalmazzo, Boves, Busca, Caraglio, Centallo, Chiusa, Coni, San-Damiano-de-Coni, Demonte, Dronero, Fossano, Limone, Peveragno, Prazzo, Roccavione, Valdieri, Valgrana, Villafalletto, and Vinadio; which are again subdivided into 61 comune. The principal towns are the cap. of the same name, Boves, Borgo, San - Dalmazzo, Peveragno, Demonte, Caraglio, Centallo, Busca, Dronero, Chiusa-di-Cuneo, and Fossano.—The government or division of C. comprises the 4 prov. of C., Alba, Mondovi, and Saluzzo. It has an area of 2,053 Italian sq. m., with a pop. in 1846 of 566,000.

CONI, the capital of the above prov., and the seat of government, is situated on a hill near the confluence of the Stura and the Zezzo, 123 m. SSE from Saluces, and 53 m. S of Turin. It was created in 1817 the see of a bishop, suffragan to the archbishop of Turin, and the seat of a prefecture of justice under the jurisdiction of the senate of Turin. It has a theological seminary and a royal college, a town-hall, a military arsenal, a foundling-hospital, 3 churches, and 7 convents. The town is surrounded by a wall having two gates. The principal street is ornamented by a colonnade and watered by a running stream. It has silk and woollen manufactories; conducts an active commerce in grain, hemp, and silk; and is the depot of goods from Nice on their way to Lombardy, Switzerland, and Germany. It has 2 fairs in the year. The pop. in 1839 was 18,777.—C. was formerly a place of great strength, and has sustained many sieges. It was taken by the French in 1796, and by the Austrians in 1799. After the battle of Marengo, it was retaken by the French, who razed its fortifications, and converted them into fine promenades.

CONIL, a large fishing town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 20 m. SSE of Cadiz, partido of Chiclana, on the coast, in N lat. 36° 15', W long. 6° 5'. Pop. 1,542. The tunny fishery is actively conducted here. It lasts from March to July.
CONINGSBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 8 m. SSW of Horncastle. Area 5,560 acres. Pop. 1,959.
CONINGTON, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 3 m. S of St. Ives. Area 1,477 acres. Pop. 169.
CONINGTON-WITH-HANTS, a parish in Huntingdonshire, 3 m. SSE of Stilton. Area 3,000 acres. Pop. 219.
CONINGSBROUGH, a parish and village in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NE of Rotherham, on the S bank of the Don. Area 4,000 acres. Pop. 1,445. There are the remains of a fine old castle here.

CONISCLIFFE, a parish in the co. palatine of Durham, 41 m. W of Darlington, on the N bank of the Tees. It is divided into the two townships of CON High C. and Low C. Area of High C. 1,700 acres. Pop. with the hamlet of Carlbury, 288. Area of Low C. 1,250. Pop. 134.
CONISHOLM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 7 m. NE of Louth. Area 1,680 acres. Pop. 166.
CONISTON, a township in the p. of Swine, E. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. NE of Kingston-upon-Hull. Area 600 acres. Pop. 116.
CONISTON-WITH-KILNSAY, a chapelry in Burnsall p., in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 11 m. E by N of Settle, on the Wharfe. Area 5,380 acres. Pop. 116.
CONISTON, or COLD CONISTON, a township in the p. of Gargrave, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. W by N of Skipton, on the river Aire. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 242.
CONISTONE, or Monk-Coniston with Skel-
WITH, a township in the p. of Hawkshead, Lancashire, 4 m. WNW of Hawkshead. Area 5,420 acres. Pop. 470.
CONISTONE. See CHURCH-CONISTONE.

CONISTONE, a beautiful lake in the district of High Furness, in Lancashire. It is 63 m. in length; but its greatest width does not exceed 1 m. It has a depth of 40 fath., within 2 m. of its lower end. It is fed by a number of mountain-streams, and discharges its waters into the estuary of the Leven. The mountain called the Old Man of C. rises, at its N extremity, to the height of 2,576 ft.
CONITZ, a town of Prussia, in the gov. and 56 m. SSW of Danzig. Pop. 1,203.—Also a town in the principality of Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt, on the Saal river. Pop. 700.
CONITZA. See KONITZA.

CONJEVERAM, or Ka'nchiveram, a town of Hindostan, in the district of Chingleput, 42 m. SW of Madras, in about N lat. 12° 49', in a valley watered by the Wegawati. It is a large and handsome town, or rather a continuous collection of villages; and possesses several handsome temples. A large proportion of its inhabitants are cotton-weavers, whose chief manufacture consists of red handkerchiefs and turbans.

CONKAIR, or Konkeir, a town of India, in the prov. of Gundwana, on the S bank of the headstream of the Mahanaddy, in N lat. 20° 20'. It is situated at an alt. of 1,950 ft. above sea-level, and is environed by a wooded mountain-district.
CONLIE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Mans.—The cant, comprising 15 com., had a pop. of 15,053 in 1841.— The com. and town are 11 m. NW of Mans. Pop. 1,664.

CONLIE'GE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Jura, arrond. of Lons-le- Saunier.—The cant., comprising 17 com., had a pop. of 8,888 in 1841.—The com. and town, 2 m. SE of Lons-le-Saunier, had a pop. of 1,183.

CONN (Lough), a lake in the N division of co. Mayo. It extends from the vicinity of Crossmolina in the NNW to the vicinity of Foxford in the SSE. Its length is 10 m.; its maximum and medium breadth are respectively about 31 and 11 m.; its circumf., measured along the sinuosities of its shoreline, is probably about 50 m. Its area is 15,644 acres; and its surface-elevation above sea-level in the winter of 1837 was 42 ft.; in the summer of 1838, 37 ft. Its lower extremity, measuring 21 m. in length, and 12 m. in breadth, is separated from the main body by a gullet or narrow channel, spanned by a bridge, and called the Pontoon; and bears the separate or distinctive name of Lough Cullen. The larger section, or Lough C. proper, has an area of 12,858 acres; Lough Cullen has an area of 2,785 acres. Lough C. proper receives at its head the Deel river, and on its W side several mountain-torrents; Lough Cullen receives on the SW the Castlebar rivulet and another considerable streamlet; and the superfluent waters of both flow E from the foot of Lough Cullen to an almost immediate confluence with the river Moy. Green Nephin mountain stoops precipitously down to the W edge of Lough Cullen and of Lough C. proper; and the great Nephin itself, the monarch of the Mayo alps, [alt. 2,646 ft.,] shakes down from the vast summit of its stupendous height, a rolling and broken assemblage of boldly featured declivities. - There is a small Scottish lake of the same name in Perthshire, in the p. of Aberfoyle, 21 m. in length, and 3 m. in breadth.
CONNA, a village in co. Cork, 5 m. WNW of Tallow, on the river Bride. Pop. 434.

CONNAUGHT, the western, smallest, least populous, and least reclaimed, of the four provinces of Ireland. It is bounded on the W by the Atlantic; on the N by the Atlantic and by Donegal bay; on the E by Ulster and Leinster; on the SE by Munster; and on the S by Munster, Galway bay, and the Atlantic. The ocean, and its offshoots of Donegal and Galway bays, form the boundary, without including secondary and minor sinuosities, over an outline of 220 m.; an artificial line, interrupted and partially superseded by Lough Melvin, the Loughs Macnean, and some unimportant water-courses, forms the boundary from Donegal bay 54 m. S to the lower extremity of co. Leitrim; the Shannon continues the boundary 62 m. to a point of Lough Derg, a little N of Scariff; and an artificial line, partially and uncontinuously superseded by lakes and rivulets, forms the boundary 34 m. WNW to the bay of Galway. The circumf., following only great sinuosities, is 370 m.; but, measured in straight lines, from projection to projection, it is only about 310 m.; while, measured along sinuosities, even exclusive of islands, it is probably upwards of 500 m. The greatest length, nearly N and S, from Bundoran on Donegal bay, to the extremity of co. Galway on Lough Derg, is 86 m.; the greatest breadth, due W from the Shannon, 21 m. below Athlone to Hyne-head, in Cunnemara, is 81 m.; and the area comprises 2,220,960 acres of arable land, 1,906,002 of uncultivated land, 48,340 of plantations, 3,877 of towns, and 212,864 of water,—in all 4,392,043 acres.—A large aggregate area on the W is insular and peninsular. But the islands and peninsulæ, for the most part, are interlaced, and jointly form an exceedingly intricate and profusely intersected broad belt of coast.—The summit level or central watershed is a little W of Ballyhaunis, and nearly at the centre of the province.—In 1841, the total of farms of from 1 acre to 5 acres, was 99,918; of from 5 to 15 acres, 45,221; of from 15 to 30 acres, 5,790; and of npwards of 30 acres, 4,275. In 1847 the number of holdings of from 1 to 5 acres was 35,597; from above 5 to 15 acres, 76,535; from above 15 to 30 acres, 33,744; above 30 acres, 24,035.—The total of the several classes of live stock, together with their respective value, were in 1841, 68,013 horses and mules, £544,104; 29,409 asses, £29,409; 295,840 cattle, £1,917,756; 525,788 sheep, £578,366; 170,922 pigs, £213,653; and 1,397,056 poultry, £34,925. Grand total of value of live stock in 1841, £3,318,213.—In 1847, the extent of land under corn and beans was 390,463 acres, of which 294,693 acres were oats; of land under green crops, 74,532 acres, of which 29,417 were potatoes.—The great district of the Western highlands of C. was, at a recent date, almost everywhere inaccessible to a wheeled carriage, and even yet contains large tracts which continue utterly impracticable; yet it has been extensively opened up by excellent roads, and is undergoing exactly such a process of rapid and ameliorating revolution as fol- | the dep. of Gard, cant. of Bagnols. Pop. 1,010. CON lowed the construction of roads throughout the Highlands of Scotland. Lines of railway, all by way of Athlone, were recently proposed to be constructed from Dublin to the town of Sligo in the NE, to Blacksod bay in the NW, and to Roundstone bay in the SE; but they do not seem likely, for the present at least, to be carried into execution. Shannon navigation is enjoyed up to the head of Lough Allen; the artificial navigation of the Grand canal to Ballinasloe; and the natural navigation of the Moy to Ballina. C. is politically divided into the counties of Mayo and Galway on the W, and those of Sligo, Leitrim, and Roscommon on the E. The co. of Clare was at one time included in the prov.; and seems naturally assigned to it both by equal quadrisection of the kingdom, and by the physical line of demarcation drawn in the river-course of the Shannon.-In 1824, according to Protestant returns, the number of schools in the prov. was 1,523, of scholars 71,721, of scholars connected with the Established church 9,003, of scholars connected with Presbyterian bodies 218, of scholars connected with other bodies of Protestant dissenters 113, of scholars connected with the Roman Catholic community 59,788, and of scholars whose religious connection was not ascertained 2,599. According to Roman Catholic returns, the number of schools was 1,523, of scholars 74,881, of scholars connected with the Established church, 9,293, of scholars connected with Presbyterian bodies 204, of scholars connected with other bodies of Protestant dissenters 112, of scholars connected with the Roman Catholic community 64,514, and of scholars whose religious connection was not ascertained 758.-Pop. of the prov. in 1831, 1,343,914. Males, 680,498; females, 683,416; families, 239,387. Inhabited houses, 224,638; uninhabited houses, 6,527; houses in the course of erection, 3,800.—Pop. in 1841, 1,418,859. Males, 707,842; females, 711,017; families, 255,694. Inhabited houses, 243,192; uninhabited complete houses, 6,293; houses in the course of erection, 392. Families residing in first class houses, 3,087; in second class houses, 26,570; in third class houses, 100,977; in fourth class houses, 125,058. Families employed chiefly in agriculture, 190,360; in manufactures and trade, 38,534; in other pursuits, 17,800. Families dependent chiefly on vested means and on professions, 4,838; on the directing of labour, 49,900; on their own manual labour, 194,968; on means not specified, 5,988.—C. was formerly a kingdom of the Irish heptarchy, and was ruled by the O'Conors. It continued, after the Anglo-Norman invasion, to enjoy a rude and semi-anarchical independency under chieftains descended from its ancient kings; and till 1590, when it was divided into counties, and put somewhat formally under English administration, it formed the grand asylum of the fugitive Irish in the various unsuccessful rebellions against the Crown. The O'Conors, during 200 years, tore the province with their feuds and dissensions; but in 1305, their race and name were brought near extinction in the sanguinary battle-field of Athenry. The surviving toparchs, when their territories became diminished, and their personal importance had greatly declined, assumed the distinctive appellations of O'Conor Dhune, O'Conor Ruadh, O'Conor Sligo, and O'Conor O'Phaby. About 1750, the appellation Dhune, which had for 88 years been in desuetude, was revived by Dominick O'Conor, the descendant of the O'Conor's Dhune, but was transmuted into Don.
CONNAUGHT (OLD), or CONNOUGH, a parish in co. Dublin, Leinster. It contains part of the town of Bray. Area 1,978 acres. Pop. 1,939.
CONNAUX, a commune and village of France, ia

CONNEAUT, a township in Crawford co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 12 m. W of Meadville, near a small lake of the same name. Pop. 1,536.- Also a township in Erie co., in the same state, 15 m. S of Erie, on Creek. Pop. 1,796.—Also a township in Ashtabula co., in Ohio, 220 m. NE of Columbus, at the entrance of Creek into Lake Erie. Pop. 2,442. It is a place of considerable shipping trade, exporting lumber, grain, beef, pork, flour, butter, and cheese.

CONNECTICUT, one of the United States of North America, lying between the parallels of 41° and 42° 2' N lat., and the meridians of 71° 20' and 73° 15' W; and bounded on the N by Massachusetts; on the E by Rhode-Island; on the S by Long-Island sound; and on the W by New York. It has 90 m. of coast. The line of boundary with Massachusetts is 72 m. in length; that with Rhode-Island, 45 m. Its area is estimated at 4,674 sq. m., or 2,991,360 acres; or about one-twentieth less than the united areas of Cornwall, Devon, and Dorset. C. is in general a hilly country; there are but few level tracts, yet the surface nowhere rises into considerable mountains. The Housatonic range enters the state in the NW part, and runs S along the Housatonic river to the coast. This is not properly a continuous ridge, but a succession of eminences, spurs, and branches, some of which are considerably elevated. Mount Tom, between Litchfield and Washington, is 700 ft. high. The next, proceeding easterly, is the Greenwood's range, which is not very elevated. Its summits in the N parts are covered with evergreens; the S division is more rugged and less lofty than the northern. The third is a greenstone range, broken and precipitous on its W face, with gentle declivities on the E. This range divides into branches towards the S, which end in East-Rock and West-Rock, near New-Haven; it abounds in minerals. The fourth range extends from Hartford to New- Haven, and is of the same geological character with the preceding, but is not continuous. It is of only a moderate height, but has a few bold elevations, exhibiting the more prominent features of the greenstone range. This range is called the Middleton mountains; though its separate parts have several distinct appellations. Passing E of the Connecticut river, we come to the fifth, called the Lyme range, forming the water-shed which separates the waters of the C. from those of the Thames. This range has also two branches towards the S, but in the N is a distinct and continuous ridge, highest in its N part. Bald mountain, between Somers and Stafford, is the loftiest summit of this range.—The valley of the Connecticut river begins at Middleton, and passes N through the middle of the state, being from 10 to 16 m. in width, and extending within the limits of this state a length of 30 m. This is the richest agricultural section in the state. The Farmington valley, extending from New-Haven N, is 50 m. in length, and from 3 to 5 m. wide. The valley of the Quinipiack, in Wallingford and North-Haven, is 15 m. in length, and from 2 to 5 m. in breadth, and is generally fertile. On the borders of the Housatonic are vales of considerable extent, from 1 to 5 m. in breadth, and of rich soil. The Connecticut river enters this state from Massachusetts, and flows S into Long Island sound. Its mouth is barred with sand, but it admits of a sloop - navigation to Hartford, 50 m. Its general course, after entering the state, is S; but at Middleton it bends to the SE, and continues in that direction to its mouth. It receives no tributary in C. of importance: the largest is the Tunxis or Farmington river, which rises in Massachusetts, and joins the C. on the W side, 5 m. above Hartford.—The Housatonic rises in the W part of Massachusetts, and enters this state near the NW corner, after which it runs in a S and SE course, to the sound between Milford and Stratford. The first part of its course is broken by cataracts, and its entrance is barred against large vessels. It has a sloopnavigation of 12 m.—The Thames is formed of two branches, one consisting of the Shetucket, and the other of the Yantie and Quinebaug. These streams unite at Norwich, and flow 14 m. to New London, where they enter the Sound. It has a good ship-navigation below the union of the head-streams.—The whole coast of the state lies upon Long-Island sound, which is in fact an extensive gulf or channel, 140 m. in length, and 25 m. broad in the widest part. It is somewhat narrow at the E entrance, but expands in the middle. Toward the W it contracts gradually till it joins the harbour of New York by a narrow and crooked strait. It admits of free navigation throughout its whole extent for the largest ships; but in the W strait is a dangerous whirlpool, at a spot called Hell-gate, where the current is contracted by the rocky shores, and at certain seasons of the tide the navigation is hazardous.—Near the outlet of the various rivers, along the whole extent of the Sound, are a number of small islands. Both the face of the country, and the local position of this state, correspond so nearly to the adjoining states of Massachusetts and Rhode-Island, that any difference in the climate can be hardly sensible. The winters are perhaps a little more temperate, and the summers a few days earlier, than in the W part of Massachusetts. The soil is generally good, but of various kinds. The valley of the C. has generally a strong and fertile argillaceous loam, varying in different sections from a hard stiff clay to a light sandy loam, according to the prevalence of aluminous or siliceous earth. The Quimpiack valley is generally fertile, but light and sandy in the S part. In the E portion of the state the prevailing soil is warm, strong, fertile, and excellent for grazing. The NW part is in some places cold and sterile, but is generally a good grazing country. In the W part of the state are many fertile districts.- The first two ranges of mountains beginning at the W are granitic; the next is a greenstone range, corresponding with the trap or greenstone of the old continent. The next is also a greenstone range; and the fifth, and most easterly, is granitic. At New- Haven, a region of trap or greenstone commences, which completely intersects this state and Massachusetts like a belt, passing into Vermont and New Hampshire. The C. flows through the whole extent of this district, except below Middleton, where it passes through a barrier of primitive country to the sea. The trap region passes off SW from the river, and joins the primitive near New-Haven. It is more than 100 m. in length, and varies in breadth from 3 to 25 m. Ridges of greenstone trap traverse this region lengthwise, from the sea to the N part of Massachusetts. Most of the ridges are parallel, and their W parts generally precipitous, so that in many places the country seems divided by stupendous walls. Iron ore is found in various places. Hematite occurs in the NW, and supplies several furnaces there; bogore is found and wrought in the eastern part. Copper is abundant in the greenstone mountains, and at Bristol there appears to be a copper field of wide extent. Lead occurs on the Connecticut, 2 m. from Middleton; plumbago is found in New Milford and Marlborough; and cobalt at Chatham. Porcelain clay exists at New Milford and Cornwall; and limestone abounds in the western parts. Marble is obtained at New Milford, Washington, Brookfield, and Milford; and quarries of freestone are numerous, especially upon the C. There are chalybeate springs アンドア アンドラス つんれ アントラ ガア アントラスス・レー・プログラング・プログラング・プログラン・ファング カラ スティー・プログラング カラ スティー・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング カラス・ファング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンドラング アンド at Stafford, which are the most celebrated in New England.—The general fertility of the soil in this state, with the skill and care bestowed upon the cultivation of the land, has made some portions of it resemble a well-cultivated garden. There are, however, considerable districts which are barren and unproductive. On the whole, the farmers of C. may be ranked among the first in the country for diligence, industry, and economy. To a high degree of skill in the art of husbandry, they add equal shrewdness in turning the produce of their farms to account. The chief articles of culture are maize, rye, wheat, oats, barley, buckwheat, flax, hemp, potatoes, pease, and beans. Orchards are numerous, and cider of the best kind is exported. The fine meadows and pastures enable the farmers to support great numbers of horses, cattle, and sheep. The farms are generally small, but seldom less than 50 acres. The mulberry is much cultivated. In 1840, there were produced in this state 87,000 bushels of wheat, 33,759 of barley, 1,453,262 of oats, 737,424 of rye, 303,043 of buckwheat, 1,500,441 of Indian corn, 3,414,238 of potatoes, 426,704 tons of hay, 83,764 lbs. of hemp and flax, 471,657 lbs. of tobacco, 889,870 lbs. of wool, 17,538 lbs. of silk-cocoons, and 51,764 lbs. of sugar. The live stock within the state in 1840 of wine were made.—The vicinity of the great mart of of this state from entering within its own territory. Most of the foreign trade is therefore diverted to the New York market; but the coasting trade along the Sound is very active. The exports from C. are much the same in kind as from Rhode-Island and Massachusetts. They amounted in 1840 to 518,200 dollars; in 1848 to 501,064 d. The imports amounted in 1840 to 227,072 dollars; in 1848 to 229,310 d. The shipping owned in this state amounts to 70,000 tons. The shad fishery of C. river furnishes a large amount of shad for exportation, and has a reputation above any other. Several ships sail to the whale-fishery from Stonington, New London, Bridgeport, &c. The manufactures, taken in the aggregate, are of great value, but many of them are in the hands of the rural pop., and there are few establishments in the state. The capital employed in manufactures was estimated at 13,669,139 dollars in 1840. The C. wares are well known all over the country, and are often carried from town to town in the remotest quarters, by pedlars from the same state. The cotton and woollen manufacture has very much increased of late years; and tin, iron, brass, and wooden ware, coaches, combs, buttons, hats, boots, shoes, saddlery, paper, machinery, and agricultural and mechanical instruments, &c., are among the products of manufacturing industry. There are in the state about 150 cotton mills, with 200,000 spindles and 5,000 looms, manufacturing annually upwards of 30,000,000 yards of cloth and 3,000,000 lbs. of yarn, and consuming above 12,000,000 lbs. of cotton; and 110 woollen factories, producing yearly 350,000 yards of broad-cloth, 800,000 yards of flannel, 60,000 yards of cassimeres, 100,000 yards of satinet, 500,000 yards of carpeting, &c., and consuming 2,000,000 lbs. of wool. The annual value of cotton and woollen manufactures is about 6,000,000 dollars; of iron-ware 500,000 d.; of axes 600,000 d.; of boots and shoes 1,500,000 d.; of buttons and combs 600,000 d.; of paper 800,000 d.; of coaches and waggons 800,000 d., &c. Vast quantities of tin-ware are made in various parts of the state, and sent to every town in the Union for sale. Wooden clocks form a thriving branch of manufacturing industry in C. In ] the single town of Bristol, above 30,000 clocks are made annually, and whole ship-loads are exported at a time. Some of the clocks are of metal, and the whole bring an average price of 8 dollars each. The Farmington canal crosses the state from New- Haven to Westfield in Massachusetts, and extends to Northampton on the C., making its whole length 80 m. It joins the sea by a large basin at New-Haven; and the first part of it passes through a level country, but towards the N considerable lockage is required. The Stonington railroad terminates at Stonington harbour, within this state. The Norwich and Worcester railroad extends to Worcester in Massachusetts, 60 m., thus forming a second line of railroad between the Sound and Boston. The New- Haven and Hartford railroad, between these two cities, is 40 m. long, and it is proposed to continue it to Springfield in Massachusetts. The pop. of this state at different periods has been as follows:-In 1790, 237,946; in 1800, 251,002; in 1810, 262,942; in 1820, 275,248; in 1830, 297,711; in 1840, 309,978. There remain about 400 of the aborigines in C.- The state is divided into 8 counties, viz.: Pop. in 1830. Pop. in 1840. The capitals are Hartford on the Connecticut river, 50 m. from its mouth, and New-Haven on a bay which runs up 4 m. from Long-Island sound.—The present constitution of C. was adopted in 1818. Before that time the state was governed according to its colonial charter. The legislature, called the General assembly, consists of a senate of 12 members, and a house-of-representatives. The representatives are chosen in towns, according to numbers, and the senators by a general ticket. All elections are annual, and the qualifications for voting amount to universal suffrage. The executive branch consists of a governor, and lieutenant-governor, who is also president of the senate, both chosen by the people. There is no absolute debt due by this state. Its permanent funds amounted, on 1st April 1847, to 406,000 dollars. Its expenditure in 1838 was 86,172 dollars; in 1842, 79,627 d.; in 1846, 92,207 d.; and in 1849, 108,741 d. C. sends 6 representatives to congress. The judges of the supreme court hold office until 70 years of age.—The Congregationalists have 270 ministers, and about 30,000 communicants; the Baptists 100; Methodists 80; Episcopalians 60 ministers. There are a few Unitarians, some Universalists, Catholics, and Shakers.—C. has 3 colleges. Yale college, at New-Haven, one of the oldest in the United States, was founded in 1701, and before its establishment at New- Haven in 1717, was for a time fixed at Killingworth, and afterwards at Saybrook. It has 21 professors, and numbered 5,856 alumni in 1849. Washington, now Trinity college, at Hartford, was founded in 1824, under the direction of the Episcopal clergy. It has 9 professors, and numbered 257 alumni in 1849. The Wesleyan university, founded at Middleton in 1831, is a flourishing institution. It has 8 instructors, and numbered 327 alumni in 1849. At Hartford is an asylum for the deaf and dumb, the first of the kind established in the United States. It has ample funds derived from lands granted by congress. The indigent deaf and dumb of the New England states are sent hither at the expense of the several state governments. The cost for each pupil is about 100 d. per annum. The number in the institution in May 1849 was 205.—The state-prison at Wethersfield is a selfsupporting institution. It contained 157 convicts in March 1849. Its receipts for the year were 13,871 d.; expenditure, 11,661 d.—The number of schoolsocieties is 217; of school-districts, 1,650; of children between 4 and 16, 88,911. The school-fund belonging to the state in 1847 was 2,077,640 d.—A state normal school for the training of teachers has been recently established by the General assembly. History.] C. was originally a colony of Massachusetts. The Plymouth settlers despatched a company in 1633, who established themselves at Windsor, on C. river. The Dutch from New York had previously built a fort at Hartford, and considered the English as intruders, although Lords Say and Seal, and Brook, with other persons, had obtained a grant of the territory from the Plymouth company in England. Other settlers shortly arrived from Massachusetts, and laid the foundation of Hartford in 1636. At first the authority of Massachusetts was acknowledged; but in January 1639, a separate government and constitution were agreed upon at Hartford, and the claim of the Plymouth colony was purchased. Another colony was soon after planted at New-Haven, by emigrants from England. Both these colonies were involved in constant disputes with the Dutch settlers at New York; but in 1650 a treaty of partition was concluded, which removed all differences, and during the war between England and Holland, which immediately followed, the colonies agreed to remain at peace. A royal charter was granted to C. by Charles II. This comprehended New-Haven, but for several years the people of that settlement refused to consent to an union. Their reluctance was overcome in 1665, and from that period one government prevailed in C. James II. annulled the charter in 1686; and Andros, who had been appointed governor of New England, was sent to assume the government. He repaired with a body of troops to Hartford, where the assembly was in session, and demanded the charter. The assembly hesitated, and put off his demand till the evening, when the instrument was brought formally into the hall and laid upon the table; but the lights were suddenly extinguished as if by concert, and the charter conveyed away. It was concealed in a hollow tree in the town, which is still pointed out to visitors as the Charter oak. Andros ruled the state arbitrarily, till deposed by the people of Boston in 1689, when the charter was resumed, and the state government was administered according to its forms till 1818.

CONNECTICUT, the largest river in New England, has its source in the highlands on the N border of New Hampshire; its NW branch, or that flowing through three lakes called the C. lakes, forming the boundary by treaty between the United States and Canada to the 45° N lat. The general course of this river is S by W; dividing New Hampshire and Vermont; passing through the W part of Massachusetts, and the centre of Connecticut; proceeding in a SSE direction, and entering Long Island sound between Saybrook and Lyme. Its total length of course is about 410 m.; flowing through a fine country, and passing many flourishing towns. On its borders in New Hampshire are Haverhill, Hanover, Charlestown, and Walpole; in Vermont are Newbury, Windsor, and Brattlebury; in Massachusetts are Greenfield, Hadley, Northampton, and Springfield; and in Connecticut are Hartford, Middleton, and Haddam. It is navigable for vessels drawing 10 ft. of water to Middleton; and for vessels drawing 8 ft. to Hartford, the head of sloop-navigation. Above this it is navigable for boats carrying 10 or 12 tons in descending, and two-thirds as much in ascending, ft. ... passing many falls and rapids in canals around them, exxa[ and towed by small steam-boats to the mouth of ft. . ft ft. Wells river, in Newbury, in the state of Vermont, a distance of 250 m. above Hartford. Immediately ft. i.ft ft. above this are the Fifteen-mile falls. The whole de- 8 exxa scent of the C. between Hanover in New Hampshire, ft. . ft and Enfield in Connecticut, is 371 ft., of which 218 ft. . if ft. have been overcome by locks: viz., at South Had- ]exxa[ ley 50 ft.; Nutter's falls 71 ft.; Bellows' falls 481 ft.; Water Quechee 121 ft.; and White river 36 ft. At Enfield there is also a fall of 5 or 6 ft., around which is carried a canal 51 m. in length. The principal tributaries of the C. are, in New Hampshire, the Upper and Lower Ammonoosue, the Sugar, and Ashen- CON White, Quechee, Black, Williams, and West rivers; in Massachusetts, Miller's, Deerfield, Chickopee, and Westfield rivers; and in Connecticut, Farmington river. In some places the banks of the C. are rough and precipitous; but through a considerable part of its course there are intervals on each side, from 1 m. to 5 m. in width, little raised above the surface of the river, and portions of which are annually overflowed. Besides the steam tow-boats, steam passage-boats ply between Hartford and Springfield; and some of a larger size form a line from Hartford to the city of New York, stopping at intermediate places on the river. This river is distinguished for its shad-fisheries: the fish caught are considered of a superior quality. Some other fish are taken; salmon, which used to be common, are now rarely found in the C.
CONNELL (GREAT), a parish in co. Kildare. Area 4,847 acres. Pop. 2,212. It is intersected by the river Liffey, and by the road from Dublin to Limerick.
CONNELSVILLE, a township of Fayette co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., on the E bank of the Youghiogany river, 180 m. SW of Harrisburg. Pop. in 1840, 1,436.
CONNEMARA. See CUNNEMARA.
CONNER, a river of Jamaica, in the p. of St. Thomas in the East, which takes its rise in Pleasant hill; runs S; and falls into the sea to the E of Port Morant.

CONNERRE', a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Montfort, 16 m. ENE of Le Mans, on the l. bank of the Huîne, which is here crossed by a handsome stone-bridge. Pop. in 1846, 1,695. It possesses some manufactories of common cloth and leather.
CONNERSVILLE, a village of Fayette co, in the state of Indiana, U. S., 60 m. ESE of Indianapolis, on the W side of Whitewater river. Pop. in 1840, 596; of township 1,372.
CONNIE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of the Loiret, and after a course of 19 m., falls into the Loire, in the dep. of the Eure-et-Loir, 4 m. N of Château-dun.
CONNOBOROUGH. See CONABURY.
CONNOIRE BAY, an indentation of the SW coast of Newfoundland, 50 m. E of Cape Ray, in W long. 57° 55'.

CONNOR, a parish and village of co. Antrim. The p. comprises an area of 17,135 acres. Pop. 7,759. The village lies on the l. bank of Glenwhirry rivulet, 4½ m. SSE of Ballymena, and 16¾ m. NW of Belfast. Pop. in 1841, 265. C. was at one time a walled town, and must therefore have possessed some importance: but it is supposed to have decayed after the eruption of the Irish in 1333; and it has long been little more than a rural hamlet, though nominally the seat of a bishopric. What little trade supports it belongs more to the adjoining village of Kells and the adjacent open country than to it; and what episcopal prerogatives or designations are affirmed of it, are, as far as the village is concerned, mere fictions. The diocese of C. is alleged, but without even a show of satisfactory evidence, to have been founded early in the 6th century. But the see, up to the year 1124, cannot be traced even by the advocates of its remote antiquity; and up to the beginning of the 15th cent. it often bore the designation of Dalriada or Dalnaruigh. Pope Eugene IV. united the sees of C. and Down; and from that date onward they have been invariably united. The nett episcopal revenue of C. is returned at £2,215 4s. 4d. The chapter consists of the dean, the archdeacon, the chancellor, the precentor, the treasurer, and 4 prebendaries. The fabric used as the cathedral is the parish church of Lisburn, and is lot rivers; in Vermont, the Pasumsie, the Wells, kept in repair at the expense of that parish. The diocese measures, from N to S, 45 Irish or 57 English m.; and from E to W, 24 Irish or 301 English m. It chiefly lies in the co. of Antrim, and comprehends very nearly all that co.; but extends also into small districts of the counties of Londonderry and Down. Dr. Beaufort, estimating its area at 395,500 Irish acres, states that 382,400 of these acres are in Antrim, 3,700 in Down, and 9,400 in Londonderry. Ascertained area, 740,623 acres. Pop. in 1831, 333,471. Total of parishes, including perpetual curacies, 72; of chapelries with separate incumbents, 2; of benefices, excluding the two chapelries, 48. In 1834, the pop. consisted of 66,888 members of the Establishment; 193,261 Presbyterians; 5,924 other Protestant dissenters; and 95,545 Roman Catholics.
CONOCK, a tything in the p. of Churton, Wilts, 51 m. ENE of East Lavington. Pop. 160.
CONONLEY. See FARNHILL - WITH - CONONLEY.

CONQUES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Aude, arrond. of Carcassonne. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,716; in 1841, 5,798. The town stands on an eminence, near the l. bank of the Orbiel. Pop. in 1846, 1,654. It possesses manufactories of agricultural implements, and of some species of cloth; and has an active trade in cattle, horses, pigs, and common cloths. Fairs are held 14 times a-year.—Also a canton, commune, and town in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Rhodez. The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,891; in 1841, 7,646. The town lies in a deep valley, near the r. bank of the Dourdou, 22 m. NNW of Rhodez. Pop. 1,418. It has three annual fairs. The locality is celebrated for its wine.—Also a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 40 m. NNE of Lerida, partido and 4 m. ENE of Tremp.
CONQUEST, a township in Cayuga co., in the state of New York, U.S., 15 m. N of Auburn, on the S of Seneca creek. Pop. 1,911.—Also a v. in Miami co., in the state of Ohio, 68 m. W of Columbus.

CONQUET (LE), a commune, town, and port of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Saint-Renan, on the Atlantic, 14 m. W of Brest, and 43 m. NW of Quimper. Pop. 1,348. Chemical substances and salt from sea-weed are its chief articles of manufacture. The harbour, which is separated by a point of land on the r. from the Blancs-Sablons, is shallow at low-water; but is well sheltered, and capable of affording accommodation to 60 vessels of 160 tons burthen.
CONQUISTA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. and 44 m. NNE of Cordova, partido of Pozoblanco, in the Sierra-Morena.
CONQUISTA. See VICTORIA.
CONQUISTA (LA), a town of Spain, in Estremadura, in the prov. of Caceres, partido of Logrosan, on the r. bank of the Alcollarin.
CONQUISTAS, a town of Brazil, in the prov. of Minas-Geraes, comarca of Ouro-Preto.

CONRADSBURG, a fort of Upper Guinea, belonging to the Dutch, on the Gold coast, in the kingdom of Fantee, on a peninsula at the mouth of the Benja, and near the fort of St. George or El Mina of which it is a kind of outwork. It is of square form, with double bastions and curtains. In 1781 it was unsuccessfully attacked by the English.
CONRAGH, a parish in co. Westmeath, 4 m. E of Ballymore. Area 3,697 acres. Pop. 908.

CONSEGUINA, or Quisiguina, a volcanic mountain of Central America, in the state of Nicaragua, on the S skirts of the gulf of Fonseca. The verge of the crater, which is a 1 m. in diam., is elevated about 3,800 ft. above the mean level of the sea. The interior walls fall perpendicularly to a depth of 200 ft., CON when the bottom of the crater becomes flattish, and has a small pellucid lake in its centre. From the first rise of the mountain, not a vestige of vegetable life could be traced in 1840, though the whole region previous to the last outbreak of the volcano, on 20th January 1835, was rich pasture ground, and abounded with fine timber, particularly cedars.—Belcher.

CONSEGUINA-POINT, the SE extremity of the entrance to the gulf of Fonseca, on the coast of Nicaragua, in Central America. It is 26 m. SSE of Amapala-point, the extremity of the opposite horn of the gulf.
CONSELICE, a town of the Papal states, in the leg. and 25 m. SSE of Ferrara. Pop. 3,000.
CONSELVE, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, delegation and 15 m. S of Padua. Pop. 4,500. It contains 5 churches, and has a saltpetre work. A fair is held here once a-year.
CONSET (CAPE), a promontory of the island of Barbadoes, on the NE coast, in the parish of St. John, in N lat. 13° 12', W long. 59° 29', on the N side of the bay of the same name.
CONSIDE-AND-KNITSLEY, a township in the p. of Lanchester, co. of Durham, 4 m. from Walsingham. Area 3,440 acres. Pop. 195.
CONSTABLE, a township of Franklin co., in the state of New York, U. S., 6 m. N of Malone, drained by Trout river. The surface is level, and the soil sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,122.
CONSTABLEVILLE, a village of West Turin township, Lewis co., in the state of New York, U. S., 138 m. NW of Albany. Pop. in 1840, about 130.
CONSTANCE. See CONSTANZ.
CONSTANT (SAINT), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Maurs, on the 1. bank of the Selle.
CONSTANTI, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 4 m. NW of Tarragona.

CONSTANTIA, a district comprising three estates in the neighbourhood of the Cape of Good Hope, at the base of the range of mountains which form the Cape peninsula, 31 m. from the v. of Wynberg. It produces grapes, apples, pears, peaches, apricots, plums, and figs, in great profusion; but is chiefly remarkable for its celebrated wine, which is of two kinds, white and red, and sells on the spot for 200 rix-dollars = £15 for the half-aum or 18 English gallons. The vines are pruned and kept in the form of dwarf bushes, much resembling current-bushes, and are planted in rows about 6 ft. apart.

CONSTANTIA, a township of Oswego co., in the state of New York, 135 m. WNW of Albany, extending N from Oneida lake, and drained by Scriba creek. Its surface is level, and its soil sandy loam. Pop. in 1840, 1,476.

CONSTANTINA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 33 m. NE of Seville, partido of Cazalla-de-la- Sierra, near the Sierra-de-C., which runs between the Sierra-Morena on the NE, and the Sierra-de- Aroche on the SW, and forms a portion of the long water-shed between the basin of the Guadiana and that of the Guadalquivir. The town of C. is of ancient origin. Pop. 6,986. It has some brandy distilleries, and a considerable trade in corn.
CONSTANTINA, or CONSTANTINE. See article
ALGIERS, p. 158.

CONSTANTINE, a flourishing village of St. Joseph co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 149 m. WSW of Detroit, at the junction of Fale and St. Joseph rivers, both of which afford excellent waterpower. Pop. in 1840, 751.
CONSTANTINE, a parish and village in Cornwall, 5 m. SW of Falmouth. Area of p. 8,470 acres. Pop. 2,042.

CONSTANTINOGORSK, a fortress in the Russian prov. of Caucasus, on the l. bank of the Podkouma, 20 m. SW of Gheorghievsk. CONSTANTINOPLE,—the STANBOL, or ISTANBOL, or COSTANTINIYYEH, of the Turks, -a city of Turkey, the once splendid capital of the Eastern empire, and now that of the Ottoman empire, situated in 41° 0′ 12" N lat., and 28° 58′ 47" E long., on the sea of Marmora, and that deep inlet of the Bosporus known as 'the Golden horn,' at the extremity of a counterfort of a chain of high hills, which, running along the shores of the Black sea, the Bosporus, and the Propontis, joins Mount Hæmus at Rhodope. The formation of this counterfort on the S side is such that three-fourths of the houses of the city enjoy a noble view of the sea. Some large ravines, deepened by the rain, serve as outlets to the waters of the fountains, and divide the irregular base on which the city is built into several parts or hills, thus giving it a physical similitude to ancient Rome. Towards the land-side, or on the W, the city is defended by a ditch and a triple line of walls 4 m. in extent, the work of Theodosius II., flanked with lofty towers of various shapes, and pierced with six ivy-clad gates. The castle of 'the Seven towers' defends the point where the W wall meets that on the S side of the city. On the NE side of the triangular peninsula occupied by the main body of the city stretches the deep but narrow waters of the Golden horn, extending about 25 m. from Seraglio-point on the SE, to the kiosk of Kyat-hanah on the NW. On the third side lies the sea of Marmora and the mouth of the Bosporus. The circumf. of the city is from 12 to 14 m. The hills upon which this city is built,— its superb imperial blue-roofed mosques, surmounted by immense cupolas, and surrounded by lofty minarets, of which the chief occupy the most elevated points of the promontory,-its monuments of ancient art,-its houses, painted in different colours, and interspersed with gardens overtopped by trees of perpetual verdure,—the disposition of all the leading edifices on an ascending site,—the aspect of the harbour, ever animated by the presence of vessels of all sizes, ships and flags of all nations, and by thousands of gondolas, -lastly, the distant perspective of the country, covered with the richest vegetation,-present a coup-d'ail the most beautiful and the most imposing, perhaps, that is to be found on the surface of the globe. The plantain and cypress, in particular, give an oriental aspect to the environs of C.; and the mulberry, the mimosa of the Nile, the acacia, the Trebizond-palm, the pine, and the fig-tree, beautifully intermingle and diversify the enchanting scenery around this metropolis. From the N side of the harbour, the eye traces the longitudinal expanse of the city. Towards the S it discovers the Mysian Olympus clad in eternal snow, and, opposed to it, the oak-clad Arganthonius. Immediately behind Scutari, on the opposite side of the strait, lies the double-peaked Burgalu, from the summit of which, following the sinuous course of the Bosporus from its very mouth, the view spreads across the thickly studded towers of C. to the expanding plains of the Propontis, where it encounters the Marmoric isles, and thence stretches to the far-distant mouth of the Hellespont. The celebrated Giant's mountain rises immediately from the Asiatic shore of the Bosporus, near its uppermost narrowing, opposite to Bujukdéré. But this magnificent physical appearance, like the vast empire of which C. is the capital, is a false index to the moral life and social position of the inhabitants of a spot so favoured by the bounties of Nature. The traveller, struck by the great extent of the Ottoman empire, and by the teeming recollections of its former glory, thinks that he is about to traverse one of the most rich and powerful states in Europe; but, as soon as he has entered it, he sees only weakness, disorder, anarchy, and all the symptoms of rapid decay: the magic of the aspect of C. disappears in the same manner. The traveller approaching C. delightedly contemplates a city apparently destined by Nature to be the queen of cities; but on entering it, the illusion vanishes; he finds within it nothing but narrow, crooked, dirty, ill-paved streets, impassable for stench and dirt,-houses of disjointed planks of wood, of brick, and of mud, covered with a deceptious cement,-large spaces strewn with the blackened ashes of conflagrations, -all the intervals between the quarters occupied with tombs, -and a multitude of men on whose countenance there is seldom to be perceived a smile, or that pleasing gaiety which characterises a contented and happy people. "Amid the novelties that strike the European on his arrival in C., nothing surprises him more than the silence that pervades so large a capital. He hears no noise of carts or carriages ratthing through the streets; for there are no wheeled vehicles in the city, except a very few painted carts, called arabahs, drawn by buffaloes, in which women occasionally take the air in the suburbs, and which go only at a foot-pace. The only sounds he hears by day, are the cries of bread, fruits, sweatmeats, or sherbet, carried in a large wooden tray on the head of an itinerant vender, and, at intervals, the barking of dogs, disturbed by the foot of the passenger,-lazy, ugly curs, of a reddish brown colour, with muzzles like that of a fox, short ears, and famished looks, who lie in the middle of the streets, and rise only when roused with blows. The contrast between C. and a European city is still more strongly marked at night. By 10 o'clock every human voice is hushed, and not a creature is seen in the streets except a few patroles and the innumerable dogs, who at intervals send forth such repeated howlings, that it requires practice to be able to sleep in spite of their noise. This silence is frequently disturbed by a fire, which is announced by the patroles striking on the pavement with their ironshod staves, and calling loudly yangen-var, i. e. 'there is a fire,' on which the firemen assemble, and all the inhabitants in the neighbourhood are immediately on the alert. If it be not quickly subdued all the ministers-of-state are obliged to attend; and if it threatens extensive ravages, the sultan himself must appear, to encourage the efforts of the firemen." [Tour in the Levant. Houses and Principal edifices.] The streets of C., with the exception of one very long one, are narrow, winding, gloomy, and deserted; all the life and activity of the interior of the city is concentrated in its bazaars. So spacious are these that, if collected together, they would equal in size an extensive city. Men, horses, and carriages, easily circulate through these miles of arched and painted markets filled with all the rich and varied merchandise of the Eastern world. The materiel of C. and of its suburbs is composed of 14 imperial mosques, 200 ordinary mosques, about 300 messjids or chapels, 80 besestins or bazaars, more than 500 fountains, and about 100,000 houses. The grand mosques, of which the magnificent columns were almost all taken from the ruined temples of ancient Greece, have been constructed upon the model of the church of St. Sophia, which is imposing by its massiveness, and admirable for the grandeur of its cupolas and dome, but less elegant than the temples of ancient and modern Rome, and perhaps less interesting, with respect to boldness and lightness, than many churches of Gothic architecture. The smaller mosques and the messjids are only distinguished from private houses by their contiguity to a minaret, from the top of which the nuezzins call to prayers. The private houses are composed of light timber, the interstices being filled up with earth and ra. - 4 A Fullarian & C !
