Combustible and fragile, if they chance to escape the frequent ravages of fire, they decay naturally, and fall into ruin in about 30 years. "The best of them, of ample dimensions, gaily painted, are pleasing to the eye; and all of them, however poor, are from their form invariably picturesque. Even the most splendid of the palaces of the sultan are of the same destructible material. The preference is given to wood by the Turks, not chiefly on account of economy, but from the persuasion that it is more wholesome than stone, and also, it is said, from a feeling of humility; it being considered by them presumptuous to dwell in buildings like their mosques, made as it were, for eternity, and keeping no measure with the frailty of the occupants. The idea of the unwholesomeness of stone buildings is not, perhaps, without foundation in such a climate. The stone houses in Galata, built by the Genoese, with walls of extraordinary thickness, are of bad repute. Unless the rooms are kept warm in winter, they must be damp in the spring: so long as the walls are cold on the occurrence of a southerly wind, they will act as refrigeratories, and occasion a precipitation of moisture from the humid warm air. The thin walls of wood, on the contrary, conform more to the temperature of the atmosphere. None of the sitting-rooms of the houses have fixed fire-places or chimneys: they are heated in winter chiefly by a charcoal fire, contained in the open mangal. The mode of warming their rooms is also suitable to the manner in which they are constructed. The crevices in the wooden work allow of a certain admixture of common air and escape of carbonic acid gas, sufficient to prevent any dangerous accumulation of the gas, so that the rooms are easily warmed, and kept warm and dry, without risk of life. Were the doors and windows of Turkish rooms suddenly made air-tight and the fissures in the wood-work closed, there being no chimney to give vent to the fixed air, half the population of C. might be suffocated any winter night between sunset and sunrise." [Dr. Davy.] The customs of the East, which establish a separation between men and women, render it necessary that these edifices should be divided into two parts, which communicate with each other only by a very narrow corridor, of which one serves as a harem for the women, while the other is used for the reception of friends and strangers. Notwithstanding the great praises which the Greeks have lavished upon the beauty of C., before the epoch of its fall, it is probable that this city was then neither much better built nor more beautiful than it is at present; for the Turks, who have adopted the costume of the original inhabitants of C., and who have given to all their grand mosques the form of St. Sophia, must have been inclined to imitate the architecture of the Greeks in the construction of their private houses also. As there exists but very few edifices—with the exception of churches—whose existence is of higher date than the 16th cent., we may believe, not without reason, that the houses of the Greeks which Mahommed II. reserved to himself for his share in the conquest of the city, were generally built of wood; and that, destroyed by fires, they have reappeared over and over again, after these disasters, in the same external form and dimensions which they had before the taking of C. by the Turks .- The besestins, or public market-places, are immense corridors, narrow enough, and badly lighted, but built of stone and surmounted with arches, to preserve from the ravages of fire the merchandise left there under the watch of men appointed to take care of it. In some parts there are open spaces, for the most part irregular, in the midst of this labyrinth of houses; but the most remarkable is the Hippodrome, begun by Severus, and finished by Constantine, where the CONSTANTINOPLE. Janissaries were accustomed to hold their assemblies, when they wanted to overthrow the ministers, or to depose the sultan. The mosque of Sultan Achmet, distinguished by having six minarets, and by the extreme delicacy of its arabesque adornments, forms one side of this quadrangle. It was built in the early part of the 17th cent. The Hippodrome, which is used at present, as in the time of the Greeks, for horse-exercises, is 400 paces long, and 100 broad; its dimensions have not changed, since an obelisk of red Egyptian granite, and the lower part of a column of bronze, are still actually standing in the central line of this place, and at equal distances from its two extremities.-The Turks, turning to advantage the subterranean aqueducts constructed by the Roman emperors, have established at C. a great number of fountains, of which the varied forms approach nearer to Chinese or Indian than to European architecture. By these aqueducts water is conveyed from artificial lakes, 10 or 12 m. from the city, in what is called the Forest of Belgrade. A double tier of 40 arches joins two of the hills on which the city is built, and forms part of the aqueduct system of C. The necessity of an abundant supply of water in every quarter of the city, for the baths and frequent ablutions of the Mussulmans, has made the Turkish fountaineers industrious. But, occupying themselves with the present only, and disdaining to cast a thought upon the future which threatens them, the Turks have neglected the numerous cisterns which the Greek emperors constructed for the supply of C. in the event of a siege, and have permitted the Basilica, or great subterranean hall of 'one thousand and one columns,'-Dr. Walsh counted 672 of them, Mr. Elliott, 448—which Andreossi calculates would once have held water enough to supply the whole city for 60 days, to be transformed into manufactories for cordage and silk yarn. Each column is about 3 ft. in diam., and the distance between them is 4 yards. The square or open space next in point of extent to the Hippodrome or Etmeidan, is the Serai-Meidani, or place before the seraglio. It is of irregular dimensions, and bounded on the N by the walls and gates of the palace. Its centre is occupied by a large and handsome fountain; and its site is a portion of the space formerly occupied by the Forum Constantini, in the midst of which stood the celebrated column and equestrian statue of Justinian, erected in 539. The column, of red porphyry, and 100 ft. high, notwithstanding the loss of its capital, still remains.—The imperial residence, the New seraglio, may be said to form 'a city within a city.' Its walls are nearly 3 m. in circuit, and contain 12 gates. The total number of souls residing within it has been recently estimated at 6,000 or 7,000. It occupies the extremity of the peninsula on which the city is built, presenting the figure of a triangle, of which two of the sides are washed by the sea, and the third adjoins the metropolis itself. What is called, from its lofty gateway, 'the Sublime Porte,' is a spacious building opposite to the principal entrance to the Seraglio. The Grand vizier resides here; and all the other ministers of state have their offices within this edifice. The etymology of the name is doubtless taken from a time when all the affairs of state were conducted in the open air, at 'the Gate.'-The mosque of Saint Sophia is situated opposite to the Seraglio. It is an enormous pile of white stone, visible from the water on all sides, with a huge dome over the centre, and several smaller domes, besides 4 minarets, surrounding the central one. Its form is that of a simple rotunda inscribed within a square. One of the greatest defects of this celebrated mosque arises from want of loftiness in the dome, the diam. of which is 115 ft., while its height does not exceed 20 ft. Its interior displays an immense number of columns, the spoils of various heathen temples. Of these, eight of porphyry, are from that dedicated to the sun by the emperor Aurelian; and the same number, of green marble, verd-antique or serpentine, from the temple of Ephesus. Its exterior is sadly disfigured by the clumsy buttresses which have been built against it at different periods, to keep it from falling. Suburbs.] The suburbs of Phanar and Eyyub form part of C., and are only separated from it by the walls. Both are situated at the N extremity of the harbour. Phanar or Fanal was inhabited by the Patriarch, and the principal Greek families, and by the suite of domestics and dependents attached to them. Eyyub is peopled by Turks only, and it contains the famous mosque called by the same name, where the standard of the prophet is deposited, and whither the Ottoman sultans, after their accession to the throne, repair in order to be girded, by the chief of the emirs, with the sabre of command, the symbol of the military sovereignty. The suburbs of Casim-pasha, Galata, and Tophana, all situated along the N bank of the harbour, are inhabited, the first by the persons employed in the tersaneh or grand naval arsenal,-the second, by traders of all nations, among whom are about 40 European factors,—and the third, 'the Wapping of C.,' by cannoniers, artillery-workmen, and their families. These suburbs, placed at the foot of a hill, are less healthy than other parts of C., on account of their exposure to the S, and do not enjoy, like Pera and Saint-Dimitri, which are situated to the N on the upper plateau that commands them, the agreeable and salutary winds of the Black sea. Galata fell into the possession of the Genoese several years before the taking of C. The suburb of Scutari, the ancient Chrysopolis, situated on the Asiatic coast, is in a happy and cheerful position. The air which is breathed there is always pure and fresh, and its environs are fertile; it serves as the point of departure and arrival to all the caravans which traverse Asia Minor in their journeys to Persia, Syria, and Hindostan. Its pop. exceeds 30,000 souls; and, from its close intercourse with the capital, it ought rather to be regarded as a dependance of the capital than as an Asiatic town. See Scutari. -The suburb of Pera, where the foreign ministers reside, adjoining to and rising above Galata, presents a union of all nations; all costumes are to be seen,—all languages to be heard here. The shops and warehouses of the traders are here laid out as in London or Paris. French, Russians, English, Austrians, &c., all distinguished under the general name of Franks, and here avoiding their national hatreds and prejudices, visit each other frequently, and live like brothers. Pera is like an isle of European civilization in the midst of Asiatic barbarity. The palace of the French ambassador, which is situated on the rapid E declivity of the hill of Pera, enjoys a very beautiful view, and has a tolerably large garden. That of the Venetian ambassador-actually occupied by the Austrian internuncio-presents a tolerably beautiful front. That of Russia is a large edifice a little to the S of the French palace. The house of the English ambassador, built on the commencement of the S declivity of Pera, was destroyed by fire in 1841. On the same site a new and very handsome edifice has been erected. It stands in the midst of a square space of 5 acres, on a promontory of the ridge of Pera, overlooking the valley of Kassim-Pasha and the arsenal. It forms from the city, and from all points to the S, W, and N, a conspicuous object. On the E it is hidden by the houses of Pera occupying the crest of the hill; so that it is not seen by ships entering the Golden horn. It is an oblong of 150 ft. by 117, and resembles more nearly than any other building that of the Reform club in London, but is larger. The stone with which it is faced is of a dazzling whiteness, like marble. In its texture it resembles Portland stone, but is closer grained; and, like that, it works with great ease, and hardens with exposure to the air. This beautiful material was brought from Aghio-Georghi [St. George], 25 m. to the W of Constantinople, and 5 m. from the sea of Marmora. The quarries from which building-stone is usually brought are at Makrikeni, some 4 m. from the Seven towers on the sea of Marmora; but, probably out of jealousy, it was pretended that there was not more than enough remaining there for the sultan's palace at Beshiktash: hence the useful discovery at Aghio- Georghi. All the four sides of the English mansion are equally faced with stone, and ornamented in like manner. The building occupies about half-an-acre of the ground, leaving the rest for a garden, with coach-house and stables. The ground on which this palace is situated was presented by the Porte to England, as an acknowledgment for the deliverance of Egypt. The Golden horn.] The harbour or 'Golden horn' of Constantinople, of which the direction is from SSE to NNW, presents, throughout an extent in length of 4,000 fath., and in breadth of 3,000 fath., a tranquil and safe anchorage. A forest of masts, and the length of the port, give it the appearance of the Thames near London; and we are also reminded of the entrance to the great canal of Venice by the number and form of the boats which are seen gliding swiftly in all directions. The depth of its waters, and the good holding of the bottom, which is of clay-mud, permit of the largest ships-of-the-line approaching either bank so as even to touch the houses. Although this harbour is the receptacle of the filth and drains of the suburbs Eyyub, Casim-pasha, Galata, and Tophana, as well as of a part of those of the city itself, still one never observes in it any encumbrance or accretion: its waters always preserve the same depth. The immense works, expensive and sometimes insufficient, for clearing and cleansing harbours, which are seen in most of the maritime cities of Europe, are very unnecessary here, since nature itself performs this operation. The waters of the two streams Ali - Bey and Kyat - hanah, the Cydaris and the Barbyssus of ancient times, joining their impulsive action to that of the current of the Bosporus, perform for the harbour of C. the important service of carrying away towards the sea of Marmora the dirt and filth which might raise the bed of it, or form accretions or shallows. The Golden horn is crossed by two bridges. One of these, the lower, consists of a number of sections each supported by 4 barges. Some distance further up is a bridge supported on long rafts. Above this lower bridge, upon the N bank of the harbour, between Galata and Hasse-Keni, is the grand marine arsenal. A small bay, called formerly the Galley-harbour, is surrounded by the house of the tersana-emini, or intendant-general of the navy,—by a spacious Place, contiguous to a hill, on which is built the vast palace that serves as the residence of the Grand pasha,—by the large and magnificent barrack for the galionjis or sailors,—and lastly, by the dock-yards and the basin for repairs of vessels, constructed by M. Rode, a Swiss engineer. To the W of the house of the Galley harbour are all the naval magazines, and the quay, near which are moored the ships-of-the-line, frigates, corvettes, and brigs, that constitute the naval force of Turkey. Behind the naval magazines, in a low and damp nook, near the hill on which the palace of the Capitan pasha is built, stands the gloomy bagnio, where man seems reduced to the last degree of misery and degradation, and where the prisoner of war is joined with the malefactor. Sultan Selim, who took a more lively interest in the wants and the glory of his empire than in his pleasures and personal enjoymen s, had given up to the navy his seraglio of Ainali-Cavak, the favourite palace of Achmet III., and extensive works had already been commenced on that site contiguous to the naval arsenal; but the deposition of Selim caused the abandonment of these projects, and the Grand Seignor has resumed possession of this ruinous palace. At the W extremity of the suburb of Hasse-Keni, which is itself to the W of the seraglio Ainali-Cavak, is situated the military school. The site of this school having become too small, on account of the number of pupils and the new division of the classes, Sultan Selim granted to this institution the palace of a sultana, sister of his father, which had not been occupied for several years; but after the fall of that prince, the pupils were obliged to quit the seraglio, which is fallen into ruins, and return to their old and confined habitation. The magnificent Coumbazadjilar-kislaci or 'barrack of the Bombardiers' is situated in front of the military school, and near to the sea. It forms, by its grandeur, its magnificence, and the height of the minarets of its mosque, one of the ornaments of the extremity of the harbour. Near this barrack are the furnaces for making bomb-mortars, and the manufactories of ordnance-carriages. The suburb Tophana, placed at the E of Galata, in front of the seraglio, contains the vast barracks of the cannoniers, and all the large establishments for artillery-works. Sultan Selim had constructed magnificent barracks at Scutari, Levend-Chiflick, and near Pera: the two former edifices were destroyed by the janissaries after the death of Mustapha-Bairactar; the third has been spared, because it was not entirely finished at the epoch of this last revolution. Defences.] The canal of Constantinople, or of the Bosporus, furnishes an outlet to the waters of the Black sea, which flow rapidly towards the sea of Marmora, and from thence towards the Ægean sea, by the canal of the Dardanelles or of the Hellespont. See article Bosporus. The gulf Buyukdere, where the strait, extending itself towards the valley of the same name, widens, serves as a place of anchorage, and as a station for vessels which are about to enter or to leave the Black sea. The canal of the Bosporus is defended, near its opening into the Black sea, by the two castles of Phanar. These two forts answer badly the end for which they were designed; but the new castles of Boiraz and Caribji, constructed by Baron de Tott, at the place where the canal begins to narrow itself, are distant from each other only 500 fath., and can engage an enemy's ship with advantage, as well on account of their proximity to each other as of the commanding position of their casemated batteries. The forts of Roumeli-Cavai and of Anatoli-Cavai, have only batteries which are open, and close to the edge of the water. Situated at the foot of a hill, of which the brow is cut by a large wall of support, these batteries would be commanded by the fire of the high batteries of ships-ofthe-line, and would become the receptacle of all the shot which might strike against the wall behind. In the new system of defence of the Bosporus, there have been intermixed, at nearly equal distances, other batteries with the old castles of the canal; these batteries are sometimes rasant, and sometimes | Trieste, C., Smyrna, Odessa, the Danube, and Treelevated 7 or 8 fath. above the level of the sea. Among the guns, there are 22 Paixhans carrying shot of from 96 to 120 lbs. Several batteries have been constructed on the shores of the Buyukdere roadstead, in order to prevent a hostile squadron establishing itself in this anchorage, after having passed the upper part of the canal in spite of the defensive works.

CONSTANTINOPLE. Commerce.] The commerce of C. is comparatively of limited importance. The exports are wool, silk, opium, wax, hides, goats' hair, skins, valonia, boxwood, gums, mohair-yarn, salep, and attar-of-roses. The imports by British vessels are cotton and woollen goods, colonial produce, iron, tin, and coals. The total value of importation by British vessels in 1841 was estimated at £1,319,174.—The total number of vessels which arrived at the port of C. in 1849, including 466 steam-vessels, was 6,294, of which 1,076 were English, 2,311 Greek, 728 Austrian, 699 Russian, 546 Sardinian, 241 French, 220 Ionian, 212 Turkish, 48 Mecklenburg, 39 Neapolitan, 30 Tuscan, 21 Egyptian, 15 Norwegian, 15 belonging to the Roman states, 10 Hanoverian, 10 Prussian, 7 Netherlands, &c. Of the 466 steam-vessels arrived, 156 were Turkish, 153 Austrian, 68 English, 49 French, 39 Russian, and one belonging to Tunis. The above returns are exclusive of the coasting-trade. - The correspondent of one of our principal daily journals writes from C. in 1849: "Smuggling may be said to be a general practice with the chief part of the trading community of C. English merchants, in almost all the countries where they are established, are the largest and most successful speculators. In C. there are but three or four English houses. There is an immense trade between Great Britain and Turkey, but it is chiefly in the hands of Greeks and Armenians. An English merchant has too high a sense of honour and probity to be a party to the corruption which his Levantine competitor merely looks upon as a kind of able dealing. This system of fraud is carried into every detail. Not only is the duty at the custom-house evaded by a thousand swindling tricks, but the dishonest merchant cheats in his dealings in the market where he brings his goods for sale. Almost invariably stuffs sold in pieces are short in the measure, and bear forged marks. English manufactured goods at one time bore a very high repute throughout the East: they have now, however, completely fallen in public estimation. The native houses at C. sent trusty agents into the manufacturing towns in England: they did not want honest merchandise, they wanted imitations. Successful imitations of all the articles required were made, and were rapidly bought up in the market of C. A system of underselling once established on such principles, it became a competition in fraud between the dealers; and one imposition succeeded another till at length the bubble burst, and English manufactured goods became a drug. False marks on the goods were then introduced, and merchandise made in Leeds or Sheffield were sold as coming from France or Belgium.—The government have a considerable quantity of paper money in circulation, but it is sold at present for 2 per cent. under its nominal value. Two per cent. ajio is paid upon the new silver coinage, and about double that sum upon gold. Then rent of houses in Pera has risen to an enormous extent; £150 a-year is a common rent for a small wooden house, and stone houses are let for £400, £500, and £600 sterling. Stone houses of any size are often let out in apartments, and two small rooms generally cost £12 a-month. Indeed, C. bids fair to become one of the dearest cities in the world." There is now regular intercourse by steam-boats between bisond. For further details on the subject of the commerce of C. see article Turkey. Population.] The number of the inhabitants of C. is uncertain: we can determine only by approximate calculation the pop. of a city where no account is kept of deaths or births, and travellers of all nations are admitted without passports. Some give to C. and its dependencies a pop. of 500,000 souls; others pretend that the number of the inhabitants of this city and its suburbs exceeds 1,100,000. The daily consumption of wheat furnishes us with the means of determining the number by approximation. It is stated that 15,000 kilots of flour = 840,000 lbs., leave the public magazines-in which is deposited, for the account of government, all the grain destined for the consumption of the inhabitants of C .- and are delivered daily to a hundred principal bakers of this capital. Supposing that the daily consumption of each individual, men, women, and children inclusive, is 1 lb. of flour-which is a large allowance, considering that the Turks make great use of fruits and vegetables-this will give us 840,000 souls; if we add to this number more than 30,000 persons who receive their subsistence from the seraglio, and a number proportioned to the daily consumption of the wheat which is smuggled, we shall have a result of about 900,000 souls for the effective pop. of C. Other calculations, founded on the ordinary course of mortality, when this city is not afflicted with the plague or by other contagious diseases gives pretty nearly the same results.—This general pop. divides itself into Greeks, Armenians, Jews, Franks, and Mahommedans. Each of these nations inhabits separate quarters, and has a distinct costume and different usages. The number of foreigners resident in C. in 1848 was as follows:- Greeks, 6,000; Maltese and Ionians, 1,983; Austrians, 4,581; French, 855; Russians, 876; Persians, 657; Sardinians, 405; Neapolitans, 247; Tuscans, 211; English, 210; Belgians, 182; Prussians, 144; Spaniards, 48; Danes, 47; Dutch, 27; Americans, 24. Total, 13,467. The form of the cahouk or hat, and the colour of the boots,-which is yellow for Mussulmans, red for Armenians, black for the Greeks, and blue for the Jews, -serve to distinguish between them at the first glance. The Effendi Turk wears a snowwhite turban; the Greek, a black turban; the Armenian, a calpac of black felt; the Jew, a blue turban; the Tartar, a high taper calpac; the Nizam-Djedid, a melon-shaped head-piece; the Imam and Dervish, a grey conical felt cap; while the Frank retains his stiff are of Asiatic origin, have nearly the same manners manners. The Jews, originally from Spain, have always arises from the love of gain when it is superior to all other human considerations. The Franks live at C. as in their native country. Having nothing to fear, either from the laws or from the local authorities, by a course of capitulations which place them solely under the jurisdiction of their own ambassadors, they live very much at ease, and wear the European costume with pride as well as security, since the Russians have by their repeated victories taught the Turks to respect the Christians.—An edict was published some years ago which declared the purchase and sale of slaves in C. to be unlawful. The prohibition, however, is only operative against the Franks, under which term the Greeks are included. White male slaves are purchased for adopted sons, and female ones for wives or adopted daughters. Young females, of the principal families of Georgia or Circassia, are often intrusted to commissioners, who are responsible for their respectful treatment. They are only purchased with their own consent, and when so purchased are recognised by the Mahommedan law as wives; the portion is settled on them by law, and if the husband misuses them, or proves unfaithful, they can sue for divorce, and recover dowry. [Lynch.] Climate.] C., situated opposite the S extremity of the Bosporus, the enclosure of which, between two parallel chains of hills, forces the air to follow the rapid movement of the waters, enjoys the double advantage of having its atmosphere continually renovated and refreshed, and its accumulations of waste and pluvial waters carried away by the currents which precipitate themselves from the harbour into the sea of Marmora. A N wind prevails from April to September, and is usually succeeded by a S wind which blows during the winter. When the wind blows during the latter season from the E or NE, the neighbouring mountains occasionally appear covered with a slight sprinkling of snow. "It may be said," remarks Mr. Turner, "that there are two climates at C., that of the N and that of the S wind. The former, bringing with it the cold which it has gathered in blowing over the Black sea, gives coolness to the days of summer, and frost and snow to those of winter. The latter, coming from the S provinces across the Archipelago, often renders a December day uncomfortably hot, and is most oppressively heating in summer. The N wind is the prevailing one, blowing with little intermission from May to September, and being frequent in the other months. It is not uncommon to see a N wind blowing in the Bosporus, and a S one in the sea of Marmora. Both of them blow at times with extreme fury. It must be owned, however, that the climate of C. is, in general, mild and moderate, though it is not sufficiently hot for the growth of olive-trees; and orange and lemontrees will not thrive in the open air, but require a slight shelter. It is very seldom that the sun is obscured by the clouds, and rain is not frequent or lasting." This prevalence of the N or NE wind is very injurious to the commerce of C., for, blowing in the direction of the currents, it frequently renders C. inaccessible to ships coming from the Mediterranean or the Archipelago. The following table exhibits the medium temp. observed at Pera in 1840 and 1841, and at Bebek in the neighbourhood of C. in 1844:- The meteorologic variations in the course of the year are about 64 days rainy, 5 snowy, 5 wintry, 20 overcast, 36 variable, 15 stormy, and 220 perfectly serene. With all these advantages of climate, C. might be expected to be ignorant of the plague, which-always most active in heavy and wet weather-owes probably its origin and its renewal, as many observations have proved, to the warm and marshy places in the environs of Damietta in Lower Egypt, whence this scourge spreads itself throughout all the provinces of the Ottoman empire, particularly in autumn and spring. But the carelessness of the government, and the dominion of fanaticism and established usages, will probably preserve the germs of this destructive malady ever lurking in C. as long as this capital shall continue under its present yoke.-C. has been frequently devastated by the bursting of the sea over its bulwarks. In 1322 its violence threw down a considerable portion of the city-walls; and twelve years afterwards the adjacent country presented one wide sheet of water for a distance of 10 furlongs. Under Justinian the Great it had been previously inundated for a space of 15 m. These excesses of nature were generally the effect of earthquakes, with which this city has been often visited, and of which none were so pregnant with calamity as that of 875, when the whole of Asia was shaken to its centre, and the promontory of Laodicea engulphed in the ocean. Placed in a central position between the regions of the N and of the S,-between the people of the E and of the W, being able to communicate with them by the Euxine, the Propontis, the Archipelago, the Mediterranean, and the ocean, and by all the affluents of these seas,-presenting a vast, safe and commodious anchorage, in its harbour and the canal of the Bosporus, to all the vessels of the world, -C. seems to be destined by nature to be the metropolis of the earth. Founded by Byzas, the leader of a Megarian colony, and formerly celebrated under the name of Byzantium, this city became more populous and more important when Constantine, sensible of the immense advantages of its position, fixed his residence and the seat of the Roman empire here, in 330. That emperor gave it the name of New Rome, to make it a sharer in the glory and advantages of the ancient mistress of the world. But the name Constantinople, i. e. 'the city of Constantine,' adhered to it; and this appellation has been preserved by the Persians, the Arabs, and even by the Turks, for, in the style of the Ottoman chancery, and upon the coin of the empire, this city is still designated by the name of Constantiniah, although the Turks commonly call it Stamboul or Istamboul. Reduced to the possession of no more than a radius of some square leagues around its walls, under the reigns of the last Greek emperors, this city presented still a respectable appearance, and formed alone the Roman empire. What may be its lot hereafter—whether the capital of a vast empire or of a small kingdom, or even a city free and independent—C. will always be, in spite of all revolutions and political reverses, one of the first cities in the world. But, in addition to the political and commercial advantages which C. enjoys by its position, this city may also easily become the first of the military places of our continent. Built upon a triangular promontory, of which two sides are washed by deep waters, it is assailable on one side only. This defensive side, presenting a straight line, might be made to enjoy all the advantages of the large half-moon system of fortification; while the suburbs of Pera, Saint-Dimitri, and Galata, may form by their junction an important place of very great strength, and of excellent support to Constantinople. Their compass passing above the Turkish burial place, upon the plateau of Pera near Saint- Dimitri, and then by Etmeidan, might abut on one side on the Bosporus, behind Dolma-Bakche, and on the other side on the harbour of C., between Hasse- Keni and the seraglio of the Ainali-Cavak. "The geographical position of C. is such as rendered her the Queen of the ancient world; occupying, as she does, the central point between Europe, Asia, and Africa, she could become the entrepôt of the commerce of all these countries, and obtain over them an immense preponderance; for in politics, as in strategy, a central position always commands the circumference. Situated between two seas, of which, like two great lakes, she commands the entrance, she could shut up in them, sheltered from the assaults of all enemies, the most formidable fleets, by which she could exercise dominion in the Mediterranean as well as in the Black sea, thereby commanding the entrance of the Danube, which opens the way to Germany as well as the sources of the Euphrates, which open the road to the Indies, dictating her own terms to the commerce of Greece, France, Italy, Spain, and Egypt. This is what the proud city of Constantine could be, and this is what she is not, because, as II. CON Montesquieu says, 'God permitted that Turks should exist on earth-a people the most fit to possess uselessly a great empire." [Louis Napoleon.]—There is no city in the world which enjoys the unenviable distinction of having been so often besieged as C. From the time of Alcibiades to that of Mahommed II. it has undergone 24 sieges; the first and the last, with those of Severus, Constantine, Dandolo, and Michael Palæologus, were however the only occasions on which the assailants were not repulsed.

CONSTITUIÇÃO, or PIRACICABA, a district and town of Brazil, in the prov. and 75 m. NE of São Paulo, situated in a fine plain, on the r. bank of the Piracicaba. Pop. 2,500. The district contains timber of the largest size, and affords pasturage to great numbers of cattle.

CONSUEGRA, an ancient town of Spain, the Consaburum of the Romans, in New Castile, in the prov. and 38 m. SE of Toledo, partido of Madridejos, on an eminence on the r. bank of the Amargullo, at the junction of several great roads. Pop. 5,124. It contains several churches and convents, a customhouse, and a bank; and has some manufactories of sacking, and common woollen fabrics. In the environs are quarries of marble, and on an adjacent hill commanding the town are the ruins of an ancient fortress.
CONSUMAHGUNGE, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district of Rungpore, 20 m. N of Dinagepoor, on the l. bank of the Teista.

CONTAI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district of Hooghly, 15 m. WSW of Injelee, and 70 m. SW of Calcutta, on a hill commanding a fine view of the sea, from which it is about 6 m. distant. Salt-works have been established here.

CONTAMINES (LES), a village of Savoy, in the prov. of Faucigny, on the r. bank of the Arve, in the Val Montjoie, at the western base of Mont Blanc, and on the road from the Col-du-Bonhomme to Saint- Maurice. Pop. 1,000.

CONTANAGOR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bengal, district and 40 m. E of Rungpore, and 6 m N of Dinagepoor, on an island formed by the Teista. It was formerly a place of considerable importance, but is now chiefly noted for its temple, which is one of the finest in Bengal.
CONTARINA (LA), a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 33 m. S of Venice, district and 5 m. SE of Loreo, on the l. bank of the Po. Pop. 2,850.

CONTAS, or Jussiappe, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Bahia, which issues from the Serra da Tromba, 50 m. N of the town of Rio-de-Contas, runs SSE, receives the Rio-Brumado or Contas-Pequeno, and the San Antonio, thence bends SE until the junction of the Gavião or Cincura, whence, after running a short distance in a NE direction, it pursues an easterly course to the ocean, into which it falls at the small town of Barra-do-Rio-de-Contas, after a total course of 225 m. On the l. side it receives the Preto, Pedras, Managerú, Area, Pires, Agua-Branca, and Oricó-Guaca. Its course is rapid, and its bed in its greater extent rocky. Vessels of large size ascend about 12 m. from its mouth. Its banks are inhabited by Indians of the Pataxos tribe.

CONSTANZ, or Kostnitz, [French Constance; ITAL. Costanza, a town in the Grand duchy of Baden, the cap. of the circle of the Lake, situated on a low and level space, on the SE bank of the lake of Constanz, "where the Rhine darts out towards the Zellersee," in N lat. 47° 39′ 40″, E long. 9° 10′, about 85 m. S of Stuttgard, and 99 m. SE of Strasburg. It comprises the suburbs of Kreuzlingen, Paradise, and Petershausen. The latter, seated on the r. bank of the Rhine, is united to C. by a wooden bridge. The town of C. is well-built; but its walls are insignificant in the way of defence, and the ditch is nearly filled. The principal street though short is wide. Among its remarkable edifices are the castle, the cathedral, the church of the ancient Dominican convent, and the episcopal palace. The hall in which the great council of C. was held is now used as a warehouse. It is about 200 ft. long, by rather more than 100 ft. wide. Its roof is upheld by plain wooden columns. From the high tower of the cathedral there is a magnificent view of the town, the two lakes, and the mountains of the Vorarlberg and Appenzel. There are 4 parish-churches, a lyceum, gynasium, normal school, and a hospital. C. is also the seat of various official departments. As a place of trade it is of little importance; and the pop. in 1846 was only 6,379, of whom 5,479 were Catholics. The chief occupations are the fisheries and navigation, brewing, the culture of the vine, weaving, and watch-making; and there are a few manufactories and saw-mills in the neighbourhood.—C. is one of the most ancient towns in Germany; and in the 3d cent. bore the name of Gannodorum. It is supposed to have been founded on the site of the ancient Valeria. In the 15th cent. it had reached great opulence, and is said to have contained 40,000 inhabitants. It is memorable for the meeting of the council between 1414-18 which deposed three popes, John XXIII., Gregory XII., and Benedict XIII., -condemned the doctrines of Wickliffe,—and treacherously consigned John Huss and Jerome of Prague to the flames. Frederick of Austria was excommunicated at this council for countenancing Pope John in his attempt to escape; and, at the instigation of the council, the Swiss declared war against the prince. C. was a free imperial town of the circle of Swabia until 1548, when it fell under the ban of the empire, and was subsequently annexed to Austria, but ceded to Baden by the treaty of Presburg in 1805. C. was the seat of a bishop in 570, which was transferred to the Swiss town of Windisch. In the 16th cent. the reigning pontiff being compelled to quit C. took up his residence at Mersbourg, on the r. bank of the lake. His diocese was the largest in Germany, comprehending a great part of Switzerland, and the circle of Swabia. In 1802 the secularized bishopric was given to the house of Baden. It formed the largest portion of the circle of the Lake and Danube. Charles de Dalberg—the last prince-bishop— died in 1817.

CONSTANZ (LAKE OF), or BODEN-SEE, a beautiful sheet of water, the Brigantinus or Bodamicus lacus, and Lacus Rheni of the Middle ages, and sometimes named 'the Sea of Swabia,' belonging partly to Germany, and partly to Switzerland; extending from SE to NW, between the parallels of 47° 27' and 47° 47' N, and between 8° 55' and 9° 46' E long. It forms part of the SW boundary of Germany on the Swiss side; on the NE it touches Würtemberg and Bavaria; and on the S it has the Austrian frontier. Its greatest length from Bregenz to Sernatingen, at the extremity of the Ueberlinger arm is, according to Weiland's map [1847], 54 geog. m.; its greatest breadth, measured in a line perpendicular to its longitudinal axis, or from Rorschach to Wasserburg, is 13\ geog. m. Its alt. above sea-level is 1,335 ft. [Guibert], 1,255 ft. [Murray's Handbook], 1,095 ft. Weiss's map.] Its depth is very unequal, varying from 320 to 964 ft. The area of the lake, exclusive of its islands, is about 200 sq. m. Opposite the town of Constanz the lake divides into two branches, distinguished as the Upper and Lower lakes; the Upper is greatly the larger section. The Lower or northern part of the lake is divided into the Zeller-see, containing the vine-covered island of Reichenau, the pro- CON by the Rhine. The northern arm, containing the island of Meinau, belonging to Baden, and the villa of Lindau, built on three small islands which are connected with the mainland by a bridge 300 ft. in length, is called the Bodmer-see or Ueberlingen-see. The Rhine enters the lake at Rheineck, on the SE, and leaves it at Stein, on the NW. A number of streams, some very considerable, empty themselves into this lake; among them are the Stokach, the Great and Little Aach, the Schussen, the Argen, and the Leiblach. The water of this lake is generally smooth, and of a greenish hue. It is liable to sudden risings, occasioned by the melting of the snow from the Alps. In 1770, it rose in one hour from 20 ft. to 24 ft. above its ordinary level; these occurrences, however, are not very frequent. Sometimes the lake rises and suddenly declines without any apparent cause. This phenomenon is called the ruhss,—a word apparently identical with the English word rush. The most remarkable of these ruhsses occurred on the 25th of February 1549. The lake was frozen over in 1830, during the severe frost in January and February; but this has happened only once within the last 56 years. The greatest thickness of the ice was only 6 inches.- The Rhine flows swiftly through the lake; and the sudden gusts of wind, here called foehn, renderits navigation by sailing boats somewhat dangerous. The lake of C. contains a great variety of aquatic birds, and 25 species of fish and crustaceæ. The traffic upon it is considerable in corn, timber, cattle, wine, &c.; though the navigation is somewhat dangerous, and is likewise impeded by the falls of the Rhine at Schaffhausen. The scenery of the lake is varied and picturesque. Its banks, generally a little elevated, are adorned with towns, villages, ruined castles, wood, and meadow. There is steam-boat communication between Rorschach, Lindau, Friedrichshafen, C., Ueberlingen, and Schaffhausen.
CONTAMINE, a village of Savoy, on the road from Geneva to Bonneville, commanding a fine view of the valley of the Arve. Pop. 1,000.
CONTE. See Porto-Conte.

CONTERS, or KUNTERS, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the eant. of Grisons, jurisdiction of Ober-Halbstein, on an affluent of the Albula, 10 m. ESE of the confluence of that river with the Hinter Rhein, at an alt. of 3,874 ft. above sea-level. Pop. (Cath.) 196.
CONTES, a village of Sardinia, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 10 m. N of Nice, on a river of the same name. Pop. 1,500.

CONTESSA, a town of Sicily, in the district of Palermo, 8 m. WSW of Corleone, on an affluent of the Belici, at the foot of Monte-Genuardo. Pop. (Arnautes or Albanians) 3,018.—Also a village of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, in the sanjak and 50 m. ENE of Salonika, at the entrance of the Kara-su into the gulf of Contessa.

CONTESSA, or ORPHANO (GULF OF), an extensive gulf of the archipelago, on the S coast of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, sanjak of Salonika, the Strymonicus sinus of the ancients. It forms an embrasure 21 m. in depth, and 18 m. in breadth at its entrance, between the peninsula of Mount Athos on the W, and the island of Thaso on the E. It derives the name of C. from the ancient town of the same name, now a mere village, on its N side, at the mouth of the Kara-su; and that of Orphano from a town of that name, 4 m. E of Contessa. In the middle of the gulf the soundings are only 10 fath.
CONTEST, a commune of France, in the dep. and 7 m. SSW of Mayenne, and on the r. bank of the river of that name. Pop. 1,368.

CONTEVILLE, a commune and town of France, perty of Baden, and the Untersee, which is traversed | in the dep. of the Eure, cant. of Beuzeville, on the L. bank of the estuary of the Seine, near the confluence of the Rille, and 9 m. NNW of Pont-Audemer. Pop. 811. It has a small port. CONTHEY, GONTHEY, or GUNDIS, a parish and town of Switzerland, cap. of the dixaine of the same name, in the cant. of Valais, 4 m. W of Sion, finely situated on the Morgia, near its junction with the Rhone. Pop. 2,239. It consists of two parts, Gonthey-le-Bourg and Plan-Gonthey. The environs afford excellent wine.

CONTICQ, or CONTICH, a commune and town of Belgium, cap. of a cant., in the prov. and 9 m. S of Antwerp. Pop. 3,672. It has manufactories of brushes and hats, and of cole-seed and rape-seed oil. The railway from Antwerp to Malines has a station within 1 m. of C.
CONTIGLIANO, a town of the Papal states, in the deleg. and 4 m. W of Rieti.
CONTIGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Chateauneuf-sur- Sarthe, 19 m. E of Segré. Pop. 1,099.
CONTIGNY, a village of France, in the dep. of the Allier, cant. of Montet-aux-Moines, 17 m. S of Moulins. Pop. 1,039.

CONTIN, a large central parish of Ross-shire. It is bounded on the N by Lochbroom; on the E by Urray and Fodderty; on the S by Kilmorack and Urray; and on the W by Gairloch and Lochcarron. It measures, along the parliamentary road which passes through it, 33 m.; and it is supposed to be little less in breadth; so that, as to extent of area, it is one of the largest parishes in Scotland. It is, in general, mountainous and barren; but embosoms numerous glens and valleys, which are well-watered, and, though of light and shallow soil, are in good cultivation. The principal streams are the Conan, the Meig, and the Rasay, which all rise near the W or NW verge of the parish, traverse it eastward, form a junction, and fall under the name of the Conan into Cromarty frith, a few miles from the town of Dingwall. Among the numerous lakes in this p. are Loch-Fannaich, 12 m. long, and 1 m. broad; Loch- Chroisg, 5 m. long, and 1 m. broad; Loch-Luichart, 6 m. long, and a 1 m. broad; and Loch-Achilty, about 2 m. in circumf. Pop. in 1801, 1,944; in 1831, 2,023; in 1841, 1,770.

CONTOOCOOK, a river of the state of New Hampshire, U. S., which takes its rise in Rindge township, Cheshire co.; runs N through Hillsborough co.; and falls into the Merrimac river in Merrimac co., on the N confines of Concord township.—Also a village of Hopkinton township, Merrimac co., in the same state, on the Contoocook river.
CONTOY, or LOGGERHEAD, an island of Yucatan, in the channel of Yucatan, 11 m. N 85° E of Cape Catoche. It is about 6 m. in length. It has good anchorage due W from its N point.
CONTRARIETES, an island of the South Pacific, in the Solomon group, NNE of St. Christoval, in S lat. 10°, E long. 162° 10'.
CONTRASTA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Alava, and partido of Salvatierra, 21 m. ESE of Vittoria.
CONTRERAS ISLANDS, a group of islands, three in number, in the N Pacific, near the S coast of the prov. of Veragua, in Guatemala, in N lat. 7° 55', W long. 82°.

CONTRES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, arrond. of Blois. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,215; in 1841, 12,165. The town is 14 m. S of Blois. Pop. 2,503. It has 9 annual fairs.
CONTREVOZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ain, cant. of Virieux-le-Grana. Pop. in 1846, 1,259. CON
CONTREXEVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Vitell, in a narrow valley, on the Vaire, 16 m. SW of Mirecourt. Pop. in 1846, 673. It is noted for its mineral waters.
CONTROGUERRA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Abruzzo-Ultra, district and 19 m. NNE of Teramo. Pop. 2,500. It has a custom-house, and several important annual fairs.

CONTRONE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 12 m. SE of Campagna, on the brow of Monte-Alburno, to the E of the Calore. Pop. 2,000. In the environs is a royal castle, with fine gardens, and an abbey.

CONTUMASA', a village of Peru, in the dep. of Truxillo, on the road from Truxillo to Caxamarca. It is composed of a long street, a plaza, and a church. The surrounding district consists principally of small plains, between and on the summits of the lower ridges of the Andes, on which wheat and barley are successfully reared.

CONTURSI, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of Principato-Citra, district and 8 m. E of Campagna, on an affluent of the Sele. Pop. 3,090. It has several mineral springs. A fair is held here once a-year.

CONTWOY-TO, or RUM LAKE, a lake of British North America, in the district of the Coppermine Indians, in N lat. 65° 50', W long. 112°, which discharges itself by Cree and Burnside rivers into Bathurst's inlet.

CONTY, or CONTI, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Somme, arrond. of Amiens. The cant. comprises 27 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,898; in 1841, 12,188. The town is situated on the Seille, 15 m. SSW of Amiens. Pop. in 1846, 897. It has a paper-mill, and some hosiery manufactures, and possesses a considerable trade in corn. C. was formerly a principality, belonging to the house of Bourbon.

CONUCON (SIERRA), a mountain-range in Guayana, extending between the parallels of 3° and 3° 25', 30 m. in a NE and SW direction, through which the river Rufununi has forced a passage. It is of granitic formation and is well-covered with wood.

CONVERSANO, a town of Naples, cap. of a circondario, in the prov. of the Terra-di-Bari, 20 m. ESE of Bari. Pop. 8,730. It is pleasantly situated on a rising ground, and contains a castle, a fine cathedral, several convents, a diocesan seminary, and an hospital. The commerce consists chiefly in wine, oil, almonds, lint, and cotton, the produce of the surrounding district. A fair is held once a-year. This town is extremely ancient, and is supposed to have been of Etruscan origin.
CONVIL-IN-ELVET, a parochial chapelry of Carmarthenshire. Pop. 1,651.

CONVIS, a township of Calhoun co., in the state of Michigan, U. S., 113 m. W of Detroit. Pop. in 1840, 292. CONWAY, CONWY, or ABERCONWAY, a parish, borough, and town in the co. of Carnarvon, North Wales, beautifully situated 13 m. E by N of Bangor, on a steep slope on the l. bank of the estuary of the river Conway, where it joins the sea, and on the line of the Chester and Holyhead railway, by which it is 39 m. distant from Holyhead, and 45 m. from Chester. Pop. in 1801, 889; in 1841, 1,358. The town is surrounded by a wall 12 ft. thick, fortified with towers and battlements, all in a state of considerable preservation. The castle, erected by Edward I., presents a striking and picturesque scene of ruins, many parts of it being still well-preserved, and calculated to give the spectator a high idea of its ancient grandeur. At low water the river is here less than 50 yds. wide, and only about 8 ft. deep, and is encumbered with shoals. At high water, the width is about | 1 m. The harbour is dry at low water, and frequented only by a few coasting-vessels. A few small vessels belong to the port; and ships are occasionally repaired here. A new quay has been built; and the navigation has been recently benefited by the deepening of the channel; but the trade is not extensive; and there are no manufactures; the surrounding district is purely agricultural. Timber, lead, and slates, are exported. The imports consist of coals from Flint and Liverpool, cotton, tea, sugar, and the ordinary articles of consumption. The town-mountain, called Mynydd-y-Dea, contains schist and compact feldspar, which used to be let by the corporation for £50 to the potteries; but the working of this material has been given up. The borough is about 18 m. in circuit. The boundary act has not disturbed the ancient limits. The municipal and parliamentary borough are co-extensive. The former is governed by a mayor, 2 bailiffs, recorder, coroner, water-bailiff, and other officers; the latter is a contributory borough to Carnarvon. The number of electors registered for 1836-7 was 41; for 1847, 48. The assizes were formerly held alternately here and at Carnarvon. C. castle, erected as a security against Welsh insurrection and inroad, was completed in 1284; and from the peculiar style and excellence of its architecture, is supposed to have been constructed by Henry De Elreton, who built Carnarvon castle. C. castle, when in its perfect state, must have been one of the most magnificent fortresses of Britain. The form of the castle was oblong, and extended along the verge of the precipitous rock, the base of which is washed by "old Conway's foaming flood," which winds round two of its sides, so as to make it almost a peninsula. The inner wall of the castle fronted the town of C. Between the town and the castle run a wide and deep ditch, which was crossed by a draw-bridge. A small entrance, defended by a strong advanced work, communicated with the river by narrow winding stairs cut in the rock. The walls were of great thickness, and flanked by 8 vast circular embattled towers, each of which had a slender machicolated watch-tower rising from the top, which produced an effect of great lightness and elegance. The two towers which flanked the grand entrance were called the King's tower, and the Queen's tower; and in each of these was a beautiful oriel window. The interior of the castle was divided into two courts. The apartments which they comprised can scarcely now be traced. The great hall curved conformably to the bend of the outer wall. It was 130 ft. long, 30 ft. wide, and 20 ft. high. It had 6 narrow windows opened into the interior court, and another still larger at one end; at the other end was the great fire-place, and there was a smaller one at the side. The roof was supported by 8 arches. Beneath were extensive vaults for ammunition and provisions. The castle was taken by the parliamentary forces in 1646, The Earl of Conway, to whom a grant of it had been made by Charles I., after the Restoration, dismantled this beautiful fortress in a barbarous manner; and caused the timber, iron, lead, and other moveable materials, to be transported to Ireland for the repair of his own property in that kingdom. The iron suspension-bridge erected by Mr. Telford, between the years 1822 and 1826, with a view to supersede the dangerous ferry which formerly existed here, has its chains fastened at the W extremity into the rock beneath the castle, and at the E end into an island rock which is connected with the shore by an embankment upwards of 2,000 ft. in length. The towers on which the chains rest are built in the same style of architecture as the castle. The suspended roadway is 109 yards in length, and is elevated 18 ft. above high water.

CONWAY. . CONWAY, or CONWY, [Welsh Cyn-wy, i.e. 'Chief water,'] a river of N. Wales. It has its source in the small lake or tarn of Llyn-Conwy, among the mountains of that rugged district, where the counties of Merioneth, Denbigh, and Carnarvon meet. Speedily augmented by the Machno, the Ceirw or Serw, the Clettwr, the Lleder, the Llugwy, and other streams of the same mountain-origin with itself, it flows with great rapidity through a beautiful and romantic valley, forming the boundary between the cos. of Denbigh and Carnarvon for about 17 m. Below Limahall, it descends, by a fall of about 50 ft., into the beautiful and fertile vale of Llanwrst in Denbighshire, whence, to the sea, its course is winding and placid. From Trefeiw, 1 m. below Llanwrst, to its junction with the sea, 1 m. below the town of Conway, it is a large river, navigable for vessels of 100 tons. At Llanwrst it passes under an elegant bridge constructed by Inigo Jones; and at Conway it is crossed by a chain suspension-bridge 327 ft. in length; and by a stupendous tubular bridge to be afterwards noticed. The extreme length of this river may be about 30 m. The flood-tide runs up the C. 43 hours, and the ebb runs down nearly 8 hours, with a velocity of 5 knots on the spring-tides, and 3 on the neaps. In the early part of its course it is famous for trout; near its confluence with the sea the pearl muscle is fished. The C. was of considerable importance, even before the Roman invasion, for its pearl muscles, the Mya margaritifera of Linnæus. Suetonius acknowledges that one of his inducements for undertaking the subjugation of Wales was the pearl-fishery carried on in that river. According to Pliny, the muscles, called by the natives kregindilin, were sought for with avidity by the Romans, and the pearls found within them were highly valued; in proof of which it is asserted, that Julius Cæsar dedicated a breastplate set with British pearls to Venus Genetrix, and placed it in her temple at Rome. A fine specimen from the C. is said to have been presented to Catherine, Queen of Charles II., by Sir Richard Wynne of Gwydir; and, it is said, has since contributed to adorn the regal crown of England. The pearl-muscle is still very abundant here, and numbers of poor people make a livelihood by collecting the shells, which are purchased by an agent on the spot.—The vale of C. is one of the most celebrated scenic portions of Wales. Broad, rich and beautiful in its lower part, it contracts and becomes bolder and more romantic near Llanwrst. Mr. Roscoe gives it the preference not only over the vale of Clywd, but over that of Langollen. On the Denbighshire side the hills are neither lofty nor bold; to the W lies a large tract of rugged mountains bounded by the deep vale of Nantfrangon, through which the Shrewsbury and Holyhead road is conducted from Capel-Curig to Bangor. The principal summits on this elevated tract are Carnedd-David 3,429 ft., and Carnedd-Llewellyn 3,471 ft., not much inferior to Snowdon in height. These rise abruptly from Nantfrangon. To the E of these summits lies a considerable tract of undulating moor containing numerous small tarns, which send off small rills as tributaries to the C. The tubular bridge of the C. is a work by means of which, and of a still more gigantic bridge of the same class thrown across the Menai strait, [See Menal,] the Chester and Holyhead railway [See HOLYHEAD] is carried in an unbroken line to its terminus at Holyhead. These spots, as is well known, had already been the scenes of vast engineering operations connected with the suspension-bridges of Telford to form the great turnpike-road communication from the metropolis to Holyhead, and thence across the channel to Dublin. When it was determined that this communication should be superseded by railway, it became a matter of most serious consideration how these two openings were to be spanned, keeping in view the new conditions of stability required for railway traffic. The subject having been remitted to Mr. Stephenson, the engineer of the Chester and Holyhead railway lines, he at once rejected the principle of the suspensionbridge as inapplicable, owing to the undulations to which it was liable, and which had been proved by practice in a similar bridge for a railway across the Tees to be both inconvenient and dangerous. How far the principle might have been modified by the introduction of proper ties and braces may be a question; but in a case of such vast magnitude and importance there might still have been risk, and, on the maturest consideration, Mr. Stephenson determined to recommend the simple and bold design of a hollow rectangular tube of malleable iron, consisting of thin plates rivetted together, such as he had already tried with success on a smaller scale upon railway bridges, and which he conceived was the best form for securing not only strength, but sufficient stability and stiffness to prevent any undue oscillations or vibrations. This noble design has been most successfully carried out. The site of the bridge is close to the S side of Telford's suspension-bridge. The tube, which is rectangular in form 412 ft. in length, 15 ft. wide, and 25% ft. in height, was constructed on a huge timber platform in a curve of the river, a few hundred yards from the site of the bridge. With the aid of pontoons and a flood-tide, the whole mass, weighing upwards of 1,300 tons, was raised from the platform, floated to its place, and placed on the abutments by the power of two enormous hydraulic rams, one acting on each end. The trains are now daily passing and repassing through it, and it has ceased to be any longer a wonder in the neighbourhood, yet the stranger pauses to gaze with admiration on this extraordinary triumph of science and engineering skill, as the train enters the tube, and again emerges under the walls of the magnificent remains of Conway castle. In passing through the tube the sound of the train is peculiar, but not greatly louder than in ordinary tunnels. No sensible tremor or vibration is experienced, and the heaviest trains, when observed externally, do not produce any visible deflection. The sides of the tube, which are 251 ft. deep at the centre, consist of malleable iron plates, only & inch in thickness, rivetted together in plates 2 ft. broad and from 4 to 8 ft. long, adjusted so as that the joints may break band. At the joints, however, the strength and stiffness of these plates is greatly increased by slips of T iron, one of which is laid on the outside of the plate and the other opposite to it on the inside, face to face, and all the four surfaces are strongly rivetted together. The top of the tube consists of two separate horizontal plates running parallel to one another, 1 ft. 9 in. apart, forming together as it were a ceiling to the tube or tunnel and an external flooring on the top. These plates are $\frac{2}{4}$ inch thick, rivetted together in breadths of 2 ft. 9 in., and in lengths of 6 ft.; and between them there run 7 vertical plates longitudinally from end to end of the bridge, I ft. 9 in. high, and 4 inch thick, separating the ceiling from the floor or upper platform, and at the same time uniting them strongly together by rivets and joints; each vertical plate having a rib of angle iron on each angle running longitudinally the whole length, by which it is united into one vast cellular mass, consisting of eight separate cells or tubes, 1 ft. 9 in. square. The object of all this strength and distribution of materials is to give the necessary stiffness and strength where the compressive force acts; and on this account the top and bottom plates are merely united by butt joints with covering plates. The bottom of the tube consists of a similar frame of cells, but only 6 in number. The upper plate consists of two layers of plates, each 1 inch thick, and the under one of the same; but as these plates are intended to resist tension, besides being laid in two layers, the plates are arranged so as to break joint, and a covering plate 3 ft. long and as thick as the plate, is placed over every joint, with sufficient rivets. The plates are 12 ft. long and 2 ft. 4 in. broad, being the whole breadth of the cell. The angle iron in the bottom cells and plates is rendered continuous by covers. The top and bottom are united to the cells by strips of angle iron running the whole length, inside and out; the interior verticle angle-irons at top and bottom are curved round to increase the strength of attachment, and there are also gusset or angle-pieces rivetted on for additional strength. The rivets used vary from 1 in. to 11 in. diam., and there are about one-fourth of a million in each tube. The rivets were all put in red hot. The sectional area of the bottom-frame of cells is 508 sq. in. These are the dimensions in the centre of the tube, but the top-plates become thinner towards the end, where they are only in thick, and also the bottom plates, where they are reduced to \(\frac{1}{4}\) in. each. The side plates again get thicker towards the ends, where they are 10 16ths thick. The ends of the tube are stiffened with cast-iron frames; there are also castings in the cells for 8 ft. at the ends, and the sides also are greatly strengthened at the ends. The tube was originally curved on the top 7 inches, and was brought to the straight line as calculated on by the elasticity of the material; showing, that with its own weight of 1,300 tons, it only sunk 7 in. The one end of the tube is fast in the stone pier or abutment, the other is loose to allow of expansion, which has been found quite visible in different states of the atmosphere. Half-an hour's sunshine at one end, or on the top, will move it laterally 11 inch and vertically 2 in.; and this when the tube is loaded with 200 tons in the centre.

CONWAY, a central county of the state of Arkansas, comprising an undulating area of 1,025 sq. m., bounded on the SW by the Arkansas river, and drained by Cadron and Point Remove creeks.-Pop. in 1830, 982; in 1840, 2,892. Its cap. is Lewisburg. -Also a township of Carroll co., in the state of New Hampshire, 68 m. NNE of Concord, intersected by Halesworth. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 324. COO Saco river and its branches. It affords magnesia and fuller's earth, and has a sulphur spring. Pop. in 1840, 1,801.—Also a township of Franklin co., in the state of Massachusetts, 102 m. NW of Boston, watered by Deerfield river and one of its branches. Pop. 1,409.
CONWAY (CAPE), a promontory on the NE coast of Australia, in S lat. 20° 32', E long. 148° 54', rising to the height of nearly 800 ft.
CONWAYBOROUGH, a village of Horry district, in the state of South Carolina, U.S., 196 m. SE of Columbia, on the W bank of Waccamaw river, which is here navigable for steam-boats.
CONYBEARE (Mount), a peak at the E extremity of the Rocky mountains, on the shores of the Arctic sea, in N lat. 69° 27', W long. 139° 53'. Its rocks are of the transition series.
CONYNGHAM, a village of Luzerne co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 108 m. NNE of Harrisburg, in a romantic mountainous situation. It consists of 50 dwellings, neatly built.

CONZA, a town of Naples, in the prov. of the Principato-Ultra, district and 9 m. ESE of San- Angelo-Lombardi, on a hill, near the r. bank of the Ofanto. Pop. 1,239. Its cathedral is a handsome edifice. This town, the Compsa of the ancients, is of great antiquity. It suffered greatly from an earthquake in 1694.
COOBLY, or CUBLY, a town of Sudan, in the territory of Borgoo, 20 m. E of Boussa, on an affluent of the Niger.
COOCH-BAHAR. See CUTCH-BAHAR.

COODOONIA, a large river of Africa, in Sudan, which takes its rise 40 m. E of the town of the same name, in the district and 70 m. N of Jacoba, unites with the Makamie, and falls into the Niger, 5 m. NW of Egga.
COOGIE, a town of Sudan, in the district and 50 m. NNW of Jacoba.
COOHIE, a town of Sudan, in the district of Zegzeg, 35 m. NW of Eggebee.

COOK, a county in the NE part of the state of Illinois, on Lake Michigan, comprising an area of 864 sq. m., drained by Calumic, Chicago, and Des Plaines rivers. It presents an undulating surface, partly swampy, but containing some fine prairies and timber. Pop. in 1840, 10,201. Its cap. is Chicago.
COOK, a river of New South Wales, in Cumberland co., which flows into the N side of Botany bay. -Also a creek on the NW confines of Cook co., an affluent of Colo river.

COOK, or Coox, a central county of New South Wales, bounded on the NE by Hunter co., from which it is separated by Colo river; on the SE it is divided from Cumberland co., and on the S from Camden co., by the Warragamba; and by the Clwyd or Cox river, on the SW, from Westmoreland co. On the NE it is bounded by Roxburgh co., from which it is partly separated by Cook creek. Its surface is covered with the ramifications of the Bluen mountains, and watered by numerous streams which descend from these to the rivers which form its boundaries. The principal town is Wilberforce, situated on the Warragamba, opposite Pitt Town.
COOKAMINGALA-CREEK, a river of South Eastern Australia, which flows into the Boorowa, 5 m. S of the confluence of that river with the Lachlaw.
COOKBURY, a parish of Devon, 4 m. NE of Holsworthy, S of the Torridge river. Area 2,340 acres. Pop. 301.
COOKHAM, a parish of Berks, 23 m. NNE of Maidenhead, bounded on the N and E by the Thames. Area 6,710 acres. Pop. 3,676.
COOKLEY, a parish of Suffolk, 3 m. WSW of
COOKOPIE, a summit of South East Australia, WNW of Croker range, in S lat. 33° W long. 148° 12', visited by Mitchell in 1835.

COOK'S BAY, an indentation of the SW coast of Easter Island, in the South Pacific, enclosed on the E by Perouse-point, which lies in S lat. 27° 8' 46", W long. 109° 24' 36". It has 10 fath. at 11 cable-length from the shore; 16 fath. at 3 cables; and 23 fath. at 4 cables.—Also an indentation of the island of Erromanga, in the group of the New Hebrides, South Pacific. At its S entrance is Bunkett harbour.

COOK'S INLET, a gulf of the North Pacific, on the S coast of Russian America, to the W of the Tchukatchis peninsula, and to the N of Kodiak island, stretching between 59° and 61° 18' N lat., and terminating in Kenaisk gulf. Its entrance is formed by capes Elizabeth and Douglas, and its total depth is 66 m. It was discovered by Cook, and subsequently explored by Vancouver. The Russians name it Kenaïskaia-gouba, from the Kenaïtzi who inhabit its shores.
COOK'S ISLANDS. See HERVEY ISLANDS.

COOK'S STRAIT, or WAI-KOMA, a channel of the South Pacific, which separates the North and Middle islands of New Zealand. It is 120 m. in length, and at its narrowest point, opposite Cape Terawiti, 30 m. in breadth. It derives its name from the navigator by whom it was discovered in 1770. The tide in this strait flows N about 5 hours; and ebbs to the S upwards of 7. Strong 'races' are found in different parts of it.

COOKSTOWN, a parish in co. Meath, 13 m. ENE of Ratoath. Area 1,238 acres. Pop. 142.—Also a pleasant market-town in co. Tyrone, 41 m. NNW of Stewartstown. Pop. 3,006. Its principal trade is in linens, and in the retail supply of the surrounding district.
COOKSTOWN, a village of Washington township, Fayette co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., 197 m. SW of Harrisburg, on the E bank of Monongahela river. Pop. in 1840, about 600.
COOK VALE, a district of New South Wales, in Georgiana co., intersected by an affluent of the Lachlan.

COOLABURRA-GUNDY, a river of New South Wales, which takes its rise at the Pandora pass, to the E of Mount Parry, runs along the NW confines of Bligh co., and uniting with Talbragar river forms Erskine river.
COOLAGH, a parish in co. Tipperary, 21 m. S of Killenaule. Area 2,558 acres. Pop. 772.
COOLAGH, or COOLAGHMORE, a parish in co. Kilkenny, 3 m. SE of Callan. Area 5,504 acres. Pop. 1,621.
COOLANEY, a village in the p. of Killoran, co. Sligo, 5 m. W of Colloony. Pop. 384.

COOLATTIN, a village in the p. of Carnew, co. Wicklow. It stands on the rivulet Derry, nearly midway between Carnew and Tinehely. Pop. in 1831, 108. COOLBANAGHER, COOLBENGER, OF BALLYCOLLON, a parish in Queen's co. It contains the village of Emo, and part of the town of Mountmellick. Area 9,621 acres. Pop. 2,383.
